rotate-image.title: Rotate Image
rotate-image.description_html:
You are given an n x n 2D matrix representing an image, rotate the image by 90 degrees (clockwise).

You have to rotate the image in-place, which means you have to modify the input 2D matrix directly. DO NOT allocate another 2D matrix and do the rotation.

 

Example 1:

Input: matrix = [[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7,8,9]]
Output: [[7,4,1],[8,5,2],[9,6,3]]

Example 2:

Input: matrix = [[5,1,9,11],[2,4,8,10],[13,3,6,7],[15,14,12,16]]
Output: [[15,13,2,5],[14,3,4,1],[12,6,8,9],[16,7,10,11]]

 

Constraints:

  • n == matrix.length == matrix[i].length
  • 1 <= n <= 20
  • -1000 <= matrix[i][j] <= 1000
rotate-image.description_text: You are given an n x n 2D matrix representing an image, rotate the image by 90 degrees (clockwise). You have to rotate the image in-place, which means you have to modify the input 2D matrix directly. DO NOT allocate another 2D matrix and do the rotation. Example 1: Input: matrix = [[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7,8,9]] Output: [[7,4,1],[8,5,2],[9,6,3]] Example 2: Input: matrix = [[5,1,9,11],[2,4,8,10],[13,3,6,7],[15,14,12,16]] Output: [[15,13,2,5],[14,3,4,1],[12,6,8,9],[16,7,10,11]] Constraints: n == matrix.length == matrix[i].length 1 <= n <= 20 -1000 <= matrix[i][j] <= 1000
rotate-image.pattern_hint: The problem asks to rotate an image by 90 degrees, indicating the use of a Math and Geometry approach. The mention of 'rotate' and 'image' are key indicators of this pattern.
rotate-image.key_insight: The core idea is to use a transpose and reverse approach to rotate the image, where we first transpose the matrix (i.e., swap the rows and columns) and then reverse each row. This has the effect of rotating the image by 90 degrees.
rotate-image.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard Math and Geometry pattern in that it requires a specific sequence of operations (transpose and reverse) to achieve the desired rotation.
rotate-image.common_mistake: A common mistake is to not reverse each row after transposing the matrix, resulting in an incorrect rotation.
rotate-image.time_complexity: O(n^2) where n is the size of the image
rotate-image.space_complexity: O(1) if done in-place, or O(n^2) if a new matrix is created

solution_breakdown.intuition: The problem requires rotating an image by 90 degrees, which suggests using a Math and Geometry approach. A transpose and reverse approach can be used to rotate the image, where we first transpose the matrix (i.e., swap the rows and columns) and then reverse each row.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem connects to the Math and Geometry pattern, as it requires rotating an image by 90 degrees. The use of a transpose and reverse approach allows for efficient rotation of the image.

steps.title: Transpose Matrix
steps.detail: Transpose the matrix by swapping the rows and columns.

steps.title: Reverse Each Row
steps.detail: Reverse each row of the transposed matrix to complete the rotation.

steps.title: Get Rotated Image
steps.detail: The rotated image is the resulting matrix after the transpose and reverse operations.

steps.title: Handle Edge Cases
steps.detail: Consider edge cases such as an empty input matrix, or an input matrix with a single row or column.

steps.title: Optimize Solution
steps.detail: Consider using a single loop to transpose and reverse the matrix, instead of using separate loops, to reduce time complexity.

edge_cases: Input matrix with a single row or column

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach has a higher time complexity as it requires generating all possible rotations, making it less efficient for large input matrices.

pascals-triangle.title: Pascals Triangle
pascals-triangle.description_html:
Given an integer numRows, return the first numRows of Pascal's triangle.

In Pascal's triangle, each number is the sum of the two numbers directly above it as shown:

 

Example 1:

Input: numRows = 5
Output: [[1],[1,1],[1,2,1],[1,3,3,1],[1,4,6,4,1]]

Example 2:

Input: numRows = 1
Output: [[1]]

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= numRows <= 30
pascals-triangle.description_text: Given an integer numRows , return the first numRows of Pascal's triangle. In Pascal's triangle, each number is the sum of the two numbers directly above it as shown: Example 1: Input: numRows = 5 Output: [[1],[1,1],[1,2,1],[1,3,3,1],[1,4,6,4,1]] Example 2: Input: numRows = 1 Output: [[1]] Constraints: 1 <= numRows <= 30
pascals-triangle.pattern_hint: The problem mentions generating a triangle with numbers, which hints at using arrays to store and calculate each row. The relationship between adjacent numbers in the triangle suggests a hashing or array-based approach.
pascals-triangle.key_insight: The core idea is to understand that each number in Pascal's Triangle is the sum of the two numbers directly above it, and to use this relationship to generate each row iteratively. This can be achieved by initializing the first row and then calculating subsequent rows based on the previous one.
pascals-triangle.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard array template by requiring the generation of a specific pattern (Pascal's Triangle) rather than simply manipulating or searching through existing data.
pascals-triangle.common_mistake: A common mistake is to incorrectly calculate the values for each row or to fail to initialize the base case (the first row) correctly.

solution_breakdown.intuition: The problem can be solved by understanding the relationship between adjacent numbers in Pascal's Triangle, where each number is the sum of the two numbers directly above it. This relationship allows for the generation of each row iteratively. The base case is the first row, which is initialized as [1].
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem connects to the arrays and hashing pattern, as it involves generating a triangle with numbers and using arrays to store and calculate each row. The relationship between adjacent numbers suggests a hashing or array-based approach.

steps.title: Initialize the First Row
steps.detail: Initialize the first row of Pascal's Triangle as [1] and add it to the result list.

steps.title: Generate Subsequent Rows
steps.detail: Generate each subsequent row by iterating over the previous row and calculating the sum of adjacent numbers.

steps.title: Handle Edge Cases
steps.detail: Handle edge cases such as an empty input or a request for a specific number of rows.

steps.title: Return the Result
steps.detail: Return the generated Pascal's Triangle as a list of lists.

steps.title: Optimize the Solution
steps.detail: Optimize the solution by using dynamic programming to store and reuse previously calculated rows.

edge_cases: Request for a specific number of rows

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach is less efficient than the dynamic programming approach, as it involves recalculating each row from scratch.

sort-colors.title: Sort Colors
sort-colors.description_html:
Given an array nums with n objects colored red, white, or blue, sort them in-place so that objects of the same color are adjacent, with the colors in the order red, white, and blue.

We will use the integers 0, 1, and 2 to represent the color red, white, and blue, respectively.

You must solve this problem without using the library's sort function.

 

Example 1:

Input: nums = [2,0,2,1,1,0]
Output: [0,0,1,1,2,2]

Example 2:

Input: nums = [2,0,1]
Output: [0,1,2]

 

Constraints:

  • n == nums.length
  • 1 <= n <= 300
  • nums[i] is either 0, 1, or 2.

 

Follow up: Could you come up with a one-pass algorithm using only constant extra space?
sort-colors.description_text: Given an array nums with n objects colored red, white, or blue, sort them in-place so that objects of the same color are adjacent, with the colors in the order red, white, and blue. We will use the integers 0 , 1 , and 2 to represent the color red, white, and blue, respectively. You must solve this problem without using the library's sort function. Example 1: Input: nums = [2,0,2,1,1,0] Output: [0,0,1,1,2,2] Example 2: Input: nums = [2,0,1] Output: [0,1,2] Constraints: n == nums.length 1 <= n <= 300 nums[i] is either 0 , 1 , or 2 . Follow up: Could you come up with a one-pass algorithm using only constant extra space?
sort-colors.pattern_hint: The problem involves sorting an array of colors (represented by integers), which suggests using an array-based sorting approach, possibly with hashing to keep track of color counts. The constraint of only having three distinct colors hints at a more specialized sorting technique.
sort-colors.key_insight: The key insight is to use a variation of the Dutch National Flag algorithm, which is a three-way partitioning algorithm that can efficiently sort the array in a single pass. This involves maintaining pointers to track the positions of the different colors.
sort-colors.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard array and hashing template by requiring an in-place sorting solution with a limited number of distinct elements, which allows for a more efficient algorithm than general-purpose sorting.
sort-colors.common_mistake: A common mistake is to overlook the constraint that the solution must be in-place, leading to incorrect approaches that use extra space.

solution_breakdown.intuition: The problem can be solved by using a variation of the Dutch National Flag algorithm, which is a three-way partitioning algorithm that can efficiently sort the array in a single pass. This involves maintaining pointers to track the positions of the different colors.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem connects to the arrays and hashing pattern, as it involves sorting an array of colors and using hashing to keep track of color counts.

steps.title: Initialize Pointers
steps.detail: Initialize three pointers, one for each color, to track the positions of the different colors in the array.

steps.title: Partition the Array
steps.detail: Partition the array into three sections, one for each color, by iterating over the array and swapping elements as necessary.

steps.title: Handle Edge Cases
steps.detail: Handle edge cases such as an empty array or an array with only one color.

steps.title: Return the Result
steps.detail: Return the sorted array.

steps.title: Optimize the Solution
steps.detail: Optimize the solution by using a single pass through the array to sort the colors.

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach is less efficient than the Dutch National Flag algorithm, as it involves sorting the array using a comparison-based sorting algorithm.

remove-duplicates-from-sorted-array.title: Remove Duplicates From Sorted Array
remove-duplicates-from-sorted-array.description_html:
Given an integer array nums sorted in non-decreasing order, remove the duplicates in-place such that each unique element appears only once. The relative order of the elements should be kept the same.

Consider the number of unique elements in nums to be k​​​​​​​​​​​​​​. After removing duplicates, return the number of unique elements k.

The first k elements of nums should contain the unique numbers in sorted order. The remaining elements beyond index k - 1 can be ignored.

Custom Judge:

The judge will test your solution with the following code:

int[] nums = [...]; // Input array
int[] expectedNums = [...]; // The expected answer with correct length

int k = removeDuplicates(nums); // Calls your implementation

assert k == expectedNums.length;
for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) {
    assert nums[i] == expectedNums[i];
}

If all assertions pass, then your solution will be accepted.

 

Example 1:

Input: nums = [1,1,2]
Output: 2, nums = [1,2,_]
Explanation: Your function should return k = 2, with the first two elements of nums being 1 and 2 respectively.
It does not matter what you leave beyond the returned k (hence they are underscores).

Example 2:

Input: nums = [0,0,1,1,1,2,2,3,3,4]
Output: 5, nums = [0,1,2,3,4,_,_,_,_,_]
Explanation: Your function should return k = 5, with the first five elements of nums being 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4 respectively.
It does not matter what you leave beyond the returned k (hence they are underscores).

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= nums.length <= 3 * 104
  • -100 <= nums[i] <= 100
  • nums is sorted in non-decreasing order.

remove-duplicates-from-sorted-array.sub_pattern_name: Two Pointers Moving in Opposite Directions
remove-duplicates-from-sorted-array.pattern_hint: The problem involves removing duplicates from a sorted array, which suggests using a two-pointer technique to track unique elements and their positions. The fact that the array is sorted is a critical hint towards this approach.
remove-duplicates-from-sorted-array.key_insight: The core idea is to maintain two pointers, one for reading and one for writing, where the writing pointer only moves forward when a new unique element is encountered, effectively overwriting duplicates. This approach preserves the first occurrence of each element.
remove-duplicates-from-sorted-array.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard two-pointer template by requiring the modification of the input array in-place and returning the length of the modified array, rather than simply finding or counting elements.
remove-duplicates-from-sorted-array.common_mistake: A common mistake is to incorrectly handle the edge cases, such as an empty array or an array with all duplicate elements.

solution_breakdown.intuition: The problem can be solved by using a two-pointer technique to track unique elements and their positions in the array. This involves maintaining two pointers, one for reading and one for writing, where the writing pointer only moves forward when a new unique element is encountered.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem connects to the two pointers pattern, as it involves using two pointers to track unique elements and their positions in the array.

steps.title: Initialize Pointers
steps.detail: Initialize two pointers, one for reading and one for writing, to track unique elements and their positions in the array.

steps.title: Iterate Over the Array
steps.detail: Iterate over the array, comparing the current element with the previous unique element, and move the writing pointer forward when a new unique element is encountered.

steps.title: Handle Edge Cases
steps.detail: Handle edge cases such as an empty array or an array with all duplicate elements.

steps.title: Return the Result
steps.detail: Return the length of the array without duplicates.

steps.title: Optimize the Solution
steps.detail: Optimize the solution by using a single pass through the array to remove duplicates.

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach is less efficient than the two-pointer technique, as it involves using a comparison-based sorting algorithm to remove duplicates.

implement-stack-using-queues.title: Implement Stack Using Queues
implement-stack-using-queues.description_html:
Implement a last-in-first-out (LIFO) stack using only two queues. The implemented stack should support all the functions of a normal stack (push, top, pop, and empty).

Implement the MyStack class:

  • void push(int x) Pushes element x to the top of the stack.
  • int pop() Removes the element on the top of the stack and returns it.
  • int top() Returns the element on the top of the stack.
  • boolean empty() Returns true if the stack is empty, false otherwise.

Notes:

  • You must use only standard operations of a queue, which means that only push to back, peek/pop from front, size and is empty operations are valid.
  • Depending on your language, the queue may not be supported natively. You may simulate a queue using a list or deque (double-ended queue) as long as you use only a queue's standard operations.

 

Example 1:

Input
["MyStack", "push", "push", "top", "pop", "empty"]
[[], [1], [2], [], [], []]
Output
[null, null, null, 2, 2, false]

Explanation
MyStack myStack = new MyStack();
myStack.push(1);
myStack.push(2);
myStack.top(); // return 2
myStack.pop(); // return 2
myStack.empty(); // return False

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= x <= 9
  • At most 100 calls will be made to push, pop, top, and empty.
  • All the calls to pop and top are valid.

 

Follow-up: Can you implement the stack using only one queue?

Notes: You must use only standard operations of a queue, which means that only push to back , peek/pop from front , size and is empty operations are valid. Depending on your language, the queue may not be supported natively. You may simulate a queue using a list or deque (double-ended queue) as long as you use only a queue's standard operations. All the calls to pop and top are valid. Follow-up: Can you implement the stack using only one queue?
implement-stack-using-queues.pattern_hint: The problem asks for implementing a stack using queues, which directly hints at using queue operations to mimic stack behavior. Understanding how stacks and queues differ in their operation (LIFO vs FIFO) is key.
implement-stack-using-queues.key_insight: The key insight is to use two queues to implement the stack, where one queue is used to store the elements and the other is used temporarily to reverse the order of elements when pushing or popping, thus achieving the LIFO behavior of a stack.
implement-stack-using-queues.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard stack template by requiring the implementation of stack operations (push, pop, top, empty) using a different data structure (queues), which involves creative use of queue operations.
implement-stack-using-queues.common_mistake: A common mistake is to incorrectly implement the pop operation, failing to properly handle the case when the stack is empty or not correctly removing the top element.
implement-stack-using-queues.time_complexity: O(n) for push and pop operations in the worst case

solution_breakdown.intuition: The problem can be solved by using two queues to implement the stack, where one queue is used to store the elements and the other is used temporarily to reverse the order of elements when pushing or popping.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem connects to the stack pattern, as it involves implementing a stack using queues.

steps.title: Initialize Queues
steps.detail: Initialize two queues, one for storing elements and the other for temporary use.

steps.title: Implement Push Operation
steps.detail: Implement the push operation by adding the element to the temporary queue and then moving all elements from the main queue to the temporary queue.

steps.title: Implement Pop Operation
steps.detail: Implement the pop operation by removing the front element from the main queue.

steps.title: Handle Edge Cases
steps.detail: Handle edge cases such as an empty stack or a stack with only one element.

steps.title: Optimize the Solution
steps.detail: Optimize the solution by using a single queue to implement the stack, but this would require a more complex implementation.

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach is less efficient than the two-queue implementation, as it involves using a single queue and moving all elements to the front of the queue when pushing or popping.

middle-of-the-linked-list.title: Middle of the Linked List
middle-of-the-linked-list.description_html:
Given the head of a singly linked list, return the middle node of the linked list.

If there are two middle nodes, return the second middle node.

 

Example 1:

Input: head = [1,2,3,4,5]
Output: [3,4,5]
Explanation: The middle node of the list is node 3.

Example 2:

Input: head = [1,2,3,4,5,6]
Output: [4,5,6]
Explanation: Since the list has two middle nodes with values 3 and 4, we return the second one.

 

Constraints:

  • The number of nodes in the list is in the range [1, 100].
  • 1 <= Node.val <= 100
middle-of-the-linked-list.description_text: Given the head of a singly linked list, return the middle node of the linked list. If there are two middle nodes, return the second middle node. Example 1: Input: head = [1,2,3,4,5] Output: [3,4,5] Explanation: The middle node of the list is node 3. Example 2: Input: head = [1,2,3,4,5,6] Output: [4,5,6] Explanation: Since the list has two middle nodes with values 3 and 4, we return the second one. Constraints: The number of nodes in the list is in the range [1, 100] . 1 <= Node.val <= 100
middle-of-the-linked-list.pattern_hint: The problem involves finding the middle of a linked list, which suggests using a traversal approach. The fact that linked lists are inherently sequential hints at using a two-pointer technique.
middle-of-the-linked-list.key_insight: The core idea is to use two pointers that move at different speeds (one moves twice as fast as the other) through the list. When the faster pointer reaches the end, the slower pointer will be at the middle of the list.
middle-of-the-linked-list.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard linked list template by requiring the identification of a specific node (the middle one) rather than simply traversing the list or searching for a specific value.
middle-of-the-linked-list.common_mistake: A common mistake is to incorrectly handle the case of an empty list or a list with an even number of nodes, where the definition of 'middle' might need clarification.

solution_breakdown.intuition: The problem can be solved by using a two-pointer technique to find the middle of the linked list, where one pointer moves twice as fast as the other.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem connects to the linked list pattern, as it involves traversing a linked list to find the middle element.

steps.title: Initialize Pointers
steps.detail: Initialize two pointers, one for the slow pointer and one for the fast pointer, to the head of the linked list.

steps.title: Traverse the Linked List
steps.detail: Traverse the linked list, moving the fast pointer twice as fast as the slow pointer, until the fast pointer reaches the end of the list.

steps.title: Find the Middle Element
steps.detail: When the fast pointer reaches the end of the list, the slow pointer will be at the middle element.

steps.title: Handle Edge Cases
steps.detail: Handle edge cases such as an empty linked list or a linked list with only one element.

steps.title: Optimize the Solution
steps.detail: Optimize the solution by using a single pass through the linked list to find the middle element.

edge_cases: Linked list with only one element
Linked list with an even number of elements

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach is less efficient than the two-pointer technique, as it involves counting the number of elements in the linked list and then finding the middle element.

remove-nth-node-from-end-of-list.title: Remove Nth Node From End of List
remove-nth-node-from-end-of-list.description_html:
Given the head of a linked list, remove the nth node from the end of the list and return its head.

 

Example 1:

Input: head = [1,2,3,4,5], n = 2
Output: [1,2,3,5]

Example 2:

Input: head = [1], n = 1
Output: []

Example 3:

Input: head = [1,2], n = 1
Output: [1]

 

Constraints:

  • The number of nodes in the list is sz.
  • 1 <= sz <= 30
  • 0 <= Node.val <= 100
  • 1 <= n <= sz

 

Follow up: Could you do this in one pass?
remove-nth-node-from-end-of-list.description_text: Given the head of a linked list, remove the nth node from the end of the list and return its head. Example 1: Input: head = [1,2,3,4,5], n = 2 Output: [1,2,3,5] Example 2: Input: head = [1], n = 1 Output: [] Example 3: Input: head = [1,2], n = 1 Output: [1] Constraints: The number of nodes in the list is sz . 1 <= sz <= 30 0 <= Node.val <= 100 1 <= n <= sz Follow up: Could you do this in one pass?
remove-nth-node-from-end-of-list.pattern_hint: The problem mentions a linked list and removing a node, indicating a linked list pattern. The phrase 'from the end' suggests a two-pointer approach.
remove-nth-node-from-end-of-list.key_insight: The key insight is to use two pointers, one of which is nth nodes ahead of the other, to find the node to be removed. When the leading pointer reaches the end, the trailing pointer will be at the node to be removed.
remove-nth-node-from-end-of-list.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard linked list template by requiring a two-pointer approach to find the node to be removed.
remove-nth-node-from-end-of-list.common_mistake: A common mistake is to try to find the length of the linked list first and then remove the node, which is unnecessary and can be avoided with the two-pointer approach.

solution_breakdown.intuition: The problem requires removing the nth node from the end of a linked list, which can be solved by using two pointers, one of which is nth nodes ahead of the other. This approach allows us to find the node to be removed when the leading pointer reaches the end. The key is to maintain a constant distance between the two pointers.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem connects to the linked list pattern, specifically the two-pointer technique, which is useful for solving problems that involve traversing a linked list with a specific offset or distance between pointers.

steps.title: Initialize Two Pointers
steps.detail: Initialize two pointers, p1 and p2, to the head of the linked list, with p1 being nth nodes ahead of p2.

steps.title: Move Pointers
steps.detail: Move p1 and p2 one step at a time until p1 reaches the end of the linked list.

steps.title: Remove Node
steps.detail: When p1 reaches the end, p2 will be at the node before the one to be removed, so we can remove the next node of p2.

steps.title: Handle Edge Cases
steps.detail: Handle edge cases such as when n is equal to the length of the linked list, in which case we need to remove the head node.

steps.title: Return Result
steps.detail: Return the modified linked list after removing the nth node from the end.

edge_cases: n is equal to the length of the linked list
n is greater than the length of the linked list
the linked list has only one node

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach involves finding the length of the linked list first and then removing the nth node from the end, which is less efficient than the two-pointer approach.

copy-list-with-random-pointer.title: Copy List With Random Pointer
copy-list-with-random-pointer.description_html:
A linked list of length n is given such that each node contains an additional random pointer, which could point to any node in the list, or null.

Construct a deep copy of the list. The deep copy should consist of exactly n brand new nodes, where each new node has its value set to the value of its corresponding original node. Both the next and random pointer of the new nodes should point to new nodes in the copied list such that the pointers in the original list and copied list represent the same list state. None of the pointers in the new list should point to nodes in the original list.

For example, if there are two nodes X and Y in the original list, where X.random --> Y, then for the corresponding two nodes x and y in the copied list, x.random --> y.

Return the head of the copied linked list.

The linked list is represented in the input/output as a list of n nodes. Each node is represented as a pair of [val, random_index] where:

  • val: an integer representing Node.val
  • random_index: the index of the node (range from 0 to n-1) that the random pointer points to, or null if it does not point to any node.

Your code will only be given the head of the original linked list.

 

Example 1:

Input: head = [[7,null],[13,0],[11,4],[10,2],[1,0]]
Output: [[7,null],[13,0],[11,4],[10,2],[1,0]]

Example 2:

Input: head = [[1,1],[2,1]]
Output: [[1,1],[2,1]]

Example 3:

Input: head = [[3,null],[3,0],[3,null]]
Output: [[3,null],[3,0],[3,null]]

 

Constraints:

  • 0 <= n <= 1000
  • -104 <= Node.val <= 104
  • Node.random is null or is pointing to some node in the linked list.

Both the next and random pointer of the new nodes should point to new nodes in the copied list such that the pointers in the original list and copied list represent the same list state. None of the pointers in the new list should point to nodes in the original list. For example, if there are two nodes X and Y in the original list, where X.random --> Y , then for the corresponding two nodes x and y in the copied list, x.random --> y . Return the head of the copied linked list. The linked list is represented in the input/output as a list of n nodes. Each node is represented as a pair of [val, random_index] where: val : an integer representing Node.val random_index : the index of the node (range from 0 to n-1 ) that the random pointer points to, or null if it does not point to any node. Your code will only be given the head of the original linked list. Example 1: Input: head = [[7,null],[13,0],[11,4],[10,2],[1,0]] Output: [[7,null],[13,0],[11,4],[10,2],[1,0]] Example 2: Input: head = [[1,1],[2,1]] Output: [[1,1],[2,1]] Example 3: Input: head = [[3,null],[3,0],[3,null]] Output: [[3,null],[3,0],[3,null]] Constraints: 0 <= n <= 1000 -104 <= Node.val <= 104 Node.random is null or is pointing to some node in the linked list.

reverse-nodes-in-k-group.title: Reverse Nodes In K Group
reverse-nodes-in-k-group.description_html:
Given the head of a linked list, reverse the nodes of the list k at a time, and return the modified list.

k is a positive integer and is less than or equal to the length of the linked list. If the number of nodes is not a multiple of k then left-out nodes, in the end, should remain as it is.

You may not alter the values in the list's nodes, only nodes themselves may be changed.

 

Example 1:

Input: head = [1,2,3,4,5], k = 2
Output: [2,1,4,3,5]

Example 2:

Input: head = [1,2,3,4,5], k = 3
Output: [3,2,1,4,5]

 

Constraints:

  • The number of nodes in the list is n.
  • 1 <= k <= n <= 5000
  • 0 <= Node.val <= 1000

 

Follow-up: Can you solve the problem in O(1) extra memory space?

binary-tree-right-side-view.title: Binary Tree Right Side View
binary-tree-right-side-view.description_html:
Given the root of a binary tree, imagine yourself standing on the right side of it, return the values of the nodes you can see ordered from top to bottom.

 

Example 1:

Input: root = [1,2,3,null,5,null,4]

Output: [1,3,4]

Explanation:

Example 2:

Input: root = [1,2,3,4,null,null,null,5]

Output: [1,3,4,5]

Explanation:

Example 3:

Input: root = [1,null,3]

Output: [1,3]

Example 4:

Input: root = []

Output: []

 

Constraints:

  • The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 100].
  • -100 <= Node.val <= 100

binary-tree-maximum-path-sum.title: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum
binary-tree-maximum-path-sum.description_html:
A path in a binary tree is a sequence of nodes where each pair of adjacent nodes in the sequence has an edge connecting them. A node can only appear in the sequence at most once. Note that the path does not need to pass through the root.

The path sum of a path is the sum of the node's values in the path.

Given the root of a binary tree, return the maximum path sum of any non-empty path.

 

Example 1:

Input: root = [1,2,3]
Output: 6
Explanation: The optimal path is 2 -> 1 -> 3 with a path sum of 2 + 1 + 3 = 6.

Example 2:

Input: root = [-10,9,20,null,null,15,7]
Output: 42
Explanation: The optimal path is 15 -> 20 -> 7 with a path sum of 15 + 20 + 7 = 42.

 

Constraints:

  • The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [1, 3 * 104].
  • -1000 <= Node.val <= 1000

combination-sum-ii.title: Combination Sum II
combination-sum-ii.description_html:
Given a collection of candidate numbers (candidates) and a target number (target), find all unique combinations in candidates where the candidate numbers sum to target.

Each number in candidates may only be used once in the combination.

Note: The solution set must not contain duplicate combinations.

 

Example 1:

Input: candidates = [10,1,2,7,6,1,5], target = 8
Output: 
[
[1,1,6],
[1,2,5],
[1,7],
[2,6]
]

Example 2:

Input: candidates = [2,5,2,1,2], target = 5
Output: 
[
[1,2,2],
[5]
]

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= candidates.length <= 100
  • 1 <= candidates[i] <= 50
  • 1 <= target <= 30

Example 1: Input: candidates = [10,1,2,7,6,1,5], target = 8 Output: [ [1,1,6], [1,2,5], [1,7], [2,6] ] Example 2: Input: candidates = [2,5,2,1,2], target = 5 Output: [ [1,2,2], [5] ] Constraints: 1 <= candidates.length <= 100 1 <= candidates[i] <= 50 1 <= target <= 30

n-queens.title: N Queens
n-queens.description_html:
The n-queens puzzle is the problem of placing n queens on an n x n chessboard such that no two queens attack each other.

Given an integer n, return all distinct solutions to the n-queens puzzle. You may return the answer in any order.

Each solution contains a distinct board configuration of the n-queens' placement, where 'Q' and '.' both indicate a queen and an empty space, respectively.

 

Example 1:

Input: n = 4
Output: [[".Q..","...Q","Q...","..Q."],["..Q.","Q...","...Q",".Q.."]]
Explanation: There exist two distinct solutions to the 4-queens puzzle as shown above

Example 2:

Input: n = 1
Output: [["Q"]]

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= n <= 9

longest-palindromic-substring.title: Longest Palindromic Substring
longest-palindromic-substring.description_html:
Given a string s, return the longest palindromic substring in s.

 

Example 1:

Input: s = "babad"
Output: "bab"
Explanation: "aba" is also a valid answer.

Example 2:

Input: s = "cbbd"
Output: "bb"

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= s.length <= 1000
  • s consist of only digits and English letters.

set-matrix-zeroes.title: Set Matrix Zeroes
set-matrix-zeroes.description_html:
Given an m x n integer matrix matrix, if an element is 0, set its entire row and column to 0's.

You must do it in place.

 

Example 1:

Input: matrix = [[1,1,1],[1,0,1],[1,1,1]]
Output: [[1,0,1],[0,0,0],[1,0,1]]

Example 2:

Input: matrix = [[0,1,2,0],[3,4,5,2],[1,3,1,5]]
Output: [[0,0,0,0],[0,4,5,0],[0,3,1,0]]

 

Constraints:

  • m == matrix.length
  • n == matrix[0].length
  • 1 <= m, n <= 200
  • -231 <= matrix[i][j] <= 231 - 1

 

Follow up:

  • A straightforward solution using O(mn) space is probably a bad idea.
  • A simple improvement uses O(m + n) space, but still not the best solution.
  • Could you devise a constant space solution?

powx-n.title: Pow(x, n)
powx-n.description_html:
Implement pow(x, n), which calculates x raised to the power n (i.e., xn).

 

Example 1:

Input: x = 2.00000, n = 10
Output: 1024.00000

Example 2:

Input: x = 2.10000, n = 3
Output: 9.26100

Example 3:

Input: x = 2.00000, n = -2
Output: 0.25000
Explanation: 2-2 = 1/22 = 1/4 = 0.25

 

Constraints:

  • -100.0 < x < 100.0
  • -231 <= n <= 231-1
  • n is an integer.
  • Either x is not zero or n > 0.
  • -104 <= xn <= 104

next-greater-element-i.title: Next Greater Element I
next-greater-element-i.description_html:
The next greater element of some element x in an array is the first greater element that is to the right of x in the same array.

You are given two distinct 0-indexed integer arrays nums1 and nums2, where nums1 is a subset of nums2.

For each 0 <= i < nums1.length, find the index j such that nums1[i] == nums2[j] and determine the next greater element of nums2[j] in nums2. If there is no next greater element, then the answer for this query is -1.

Return an array ans of length nums1.length such that ans[i] is the next greater element as described above.

 

Example 1:

Input: nums1 = [4,1,2], nums2 = [1,3,4,2]
Output: [-1,3,-1]
Explanation: The next greater element for each value of nums1 is as follows:
- 4 is underlined in nums2 = [1,3,4,2]. There is no next greater element, so the answer is -1.
- 1 is underlined in nums2 = [1,3,4,2]. The next greater element is 3.
- 2 is underlined in nums2 = [1,3,4,2]. There is no next greater element, so the answer is -1.

Example 2:

Input: nums1 = [2,4], nums2 = [1,2,3,4]
Output: [3,-1]
Explanation: The next greater element for each value of nums1 is as follows:
- 2 is underlined in nums2 = [1,2,3,4]. The next greater element is 3.
- 4 is underlined in nums2 = [1,2,3,4]. There is no next greater element, so the answer is -1.

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= nums1.length <= nums2.length <= 1000
  • 0 <= nums1[i], nums2[i] <= 104
  • All integers in nums1 and nums2 are unique.
  • All the integers of nums1 also appear in nums2.

 

Follow up: Could you find an O(nums1.length + nums2.length) solution?

steps.title: Initialize Stack and Hash Map

steps.title: Iterate Through Second Array

steps.title: Push Index to Stack
steps.detail: Push the index of the current element from the first array onto the stack if it is not empty and the top of the stack has a value less than the current element.

steps.title: Update Hash Map
steps.detail: Update the hash map with the next greater element for each index popped from the stack.

merge-sorted-array.title: Merge Sorted Array
merge-sorted-array.description_html:
You are given two integer arrays nums1 and nums2, sorted in non-decreasing order, and two integers m and n, representing the number of elements in nums1 and nums2 respectively.

Merge nums1 and nums2 into a single array sorted in non-decreasing order.

The final sorted array should not be returned by the function, but instead be stored inside the array nums1. To accommodate this, nums1 has a length of m + n, where the first m elements denote the elements that should be merged, and the last n elements are set to 0 and should be ignored. nums2 has a length of n.

 

Example 1:

Input: nums1 = [1,2,3,0,0,0], m = 3, nums2 = [2,5,6], n = 3
Output: [1,2,2,3,5,6]
Explanation: The arrays we are merging are [1,2,3] and [2,5,6].
The result of the merge is [1,2,2,3,5,6] with the underlined elements coming from nums1.

Example 2:

Input: nums1 = [1], m = 1, nums2 = [], n = 0
Output: [1]
Explanation: The arrays we are merging are [1] and [].
The result of the merge is [1].

Example 3:

Input: nums1 = [0], m = 0, nums2 = [1], n = 1
Output: [1]
Explanation: The arrays we are merging are [] and [1].
The result of the merge is [1].
Note that because m = 0, there are no elements in nums1. The 0 is only there to ensure the merge result can fit in nums1.

 

Constraints:

  • nums1.length == m + n
  • nums2.length == n
  • 0 <= m, n <= 200
  • 1 <= m + n <= 200
  • -109 <= nums1[i], nums2[j] <= 109

 

Follow up: Can you come up with an algorithm that runs in O(m + n) time?

This approach allows for a single pass through both arrays, reducing time complexity. The use of a temporary array to store the merged result enables efficient storage and retrieval of elements.

The problem's requirement for efficient comparison and merging of elements makes the two-pointer technique an ideal approach.

steps.detail: Initialize two pointers, one for each array, and a temporary array to store the merged result.

steps.title: Compare and Merge Elements
steps.detail: Compare the elements at the current positions of the two pointers and merge the smaller element into the temporary array, incrementing the corresponding pointer.

steps.title: Handle Remaining Elements
steps.detail: If one array is exhausted before the other, append the remaining elements from the non-exhausted array to the temporary array.

steps.title: Copy Merged Result to Original Array
steps.detail: Copy the merged result from the temporary array to the original array, starting from the end of the array.

steps.detail: Return the merged array, which is now sorted in ascending order.

construct-binary-tree-from-preorder-and-.title: Construct Binary Tree From Preorder And Inorder Traversal
construct-binary-tree-from-preorder-and-.description_html:
Given two integer arrays preorder and inorder where preorder is the preorder traversal of a binary tree and inorder is the inorder traversal of the same tree, construct and return the binary tree.

 

Example 1:

Input: preorder = [3,9,20,15,7], inorder = [9,3,15,20,7]
Output: [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]

Example 2:

Input: preorder = [-1], inorder = [-1]
Output: [-1]

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= preorder.length <= 3000
  • inorder.length == preorder.length
  • -3000 <= preorder[i], inorder[i] <= 3000
  • preorder and inorder consist of unique values.
  • Each value of inorder also appears in preorder.
  • preorder is guaranteed to be the preorder traversal of the tree.
  • inorder is guaranteed to be the inorder traversal of the tree.

This approach allows for a single pass through the preorder and inorder sequences, reducing time complexity. The use of indices to track the current position in the sequences enables efficient traversal.

rotting-oranges.title: Rotting Oranges
rotting-oranges.description_html:
You are given an m x n grid where each cell can have one of three values:

  • 0 representing an empty cell,
  • 1 representing a fresh orange, or
  • 2 representing a rotten orange.

Every minute, any fresh orange that is 4-directionally adjacent to a rotten orange becomes rotten.

Return the minimum number of minutes that must elapse until no cell has a fresh orange. If this is impossible, return -1.

 

Example 1:

Input: grid = [[2,1,1],[1,1,0],[0,1,1]]
Output: 4

Example 2:

Input: grid = [[2,1,1],[0,1,1],[1,0,1]]
Output: -1
Explanation: The orange in the bottom left corner (row 2, column 0) is never rotten, because rotting only happens 4-directionally.

Example 3:

Input: grid = [[0,2]]
Output: 0
Explanation: Since there are already no fresh oranges at minute 0, the answer is just 0.

 

Constraints:

  • m == grid.length
  • n == grid[i].length
  • 1 <= m, n <= 10
  • grid[i][j] is 0, 1, or 2.

If this is impossible, return -1 . Example 1: Input: grid = [[2,1,1],[1,1,0],[0,1,1]] Output: 4 Example 2: Input: grid = [[2,1,1],[0,1,1],[1,0,1]] Output: -1 Explanation: The orange in the bottom left corner (row 2, column 0) is never rotten, because rotting only happens 4-directionally. Example 3: Input: grid = [[0,2]] Output: 0 Explanation: Since there are already no fresh oranges at minute 0, the answer is just 0. Constraints: m == grid.length n == grid[i].length 1 <= m, n <= 10 grid[i][j] is 0 , 1 , or 2 .

rotting-oranges.common_mistake: A common mistake is to fail to properly handle the queue for BFS or to not keep track of visited cells to avoid revisiting them.

This approach allows for a single pass through the grid, reducing time complexity. The use of a queue to store cells to be processed enables efficient traversal.

roman-to-integer.title: Roman to Integer
roman-to-integer.description_html:
Roman numerals are represented by seven different symbols: I, V, X, L, C, D and M.

Symbol       Value
I             1
V             5
X             10
L             50
C             100
D             500
M             1000

For example, 2 is written as II in Roman numeral, just two ones added together. 12 is written as XII, which is simply X + II. The number 27 is written as XXVII, which is XX + V + II.

Roman numerals are usually written largest to smallest from left to right. However, the numeral for four is not IIII. Instead, the number four is written as IV. Because the one is before the five we subtract it making four. The same principle applies to the number nine, which is written as IX. There are six instances where subtraction is used:

  • I can be placed before V (5) and X (10) to make 4 and 9. 
  • X can be placed before L (50) and C (100) to make 40 and 90. 
  • C can be placed before D (500) and M (1000) to make 400 and 900.

Given a roman numeral, convert it to an integer.

 

Example 1:

Input: s = "III"
Output: 3
Explanation: III = 3.

Example 2:

Input: s = "LVIII"
Output: 58
Explanation: L = 50, V= 5, III = 3.

Example 3:

Input: s = "MCMXCIV"
Output: 1994
Explanation: M = 1000, CM = 900, XC = 90 and IV = 4.

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= s.length <= 15
  • s contains only the characters ('I', 'V', 'X', 'L', 'C', 'D', 'M').
  • It is guaranteed that s is a valid roman numeral in the range [1, 3999].

roman-to-integer.key_insight: The key insight is to iterate through the Roman numeral string from left to right, adding the value of each numeral to a total, but subtracting the value if the next numeral is greater.
roman-to-integer.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard mapping template by requiring a conditional subtraction based on the comparison of adjacent numerals.
roman-to-integer.common_mistake: A common mistake is to not handle the subtraction rule correctly, especially for cases like IV (4) and IX (9).

steps.detail: Handle edge cases such as an empty string or a string with invalid characters.

4sum.title: 4Sum
4sum.description_html:
Given an array nums of n integers, return an array of all the unique quadruplets [nums[a], nums[b], nums[c], nums[d]] such that:

  • 0 <= a, b, c, d < n
  • a, b, c, and d are distinct.
  • nums[a] + nums[b] + nums[c] + nums[d] == target

You may return the answer in any order.

 

Example 1:

Input: nums = [1,0,-1,0,-2,2], target = 0
Output: [[-2,-1,1,2],[-2,0,0,2],[-1,0,0,1]]

Example 2:

Input: nums = [2,2,2,2,2], target = 8
Output: [[2,2,2,2]]

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= nums.length <= 200
  • -109 <= nums[i] <= 109
  • -109 <= target <= 109

Example 1: Input: nums = [1,0,-1,0,-2,2], target = 0 Output: [[-2,-1,1,2],[-2,0,0,2],[-1,0,0,1]] Example 2: Input: nums = [2,2,2,2,2], target = 8 Output: [[2,2,2,2]] Constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 200 -109 <= nums[i] <= 109 -109 <= target <= 109
4sum.sub_pattern_name: Two Pointers Moving in Opposite Directions
4sum.pattern_hint: The problem asks for four numbers that sum to a target, indicating a need for a Two Pointers approach to find pairs that sum to a remaining target. The presence of a target sum and the need to find multiple elements that add up to it suggests using Two Pointers.
4sum.key_insight: The core idea is to fix two numbers and then use Two Pointers to find the remaining two numbers that sum to the target. Sorting the array is crucial to apply the Two Pointers technique.
4sum.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard Two Pointers template by requiring a nested loop to fix two numbers before applying the Two Pointers technique.
4sum.common_mistake: A common mistake is not skipping duplicate quadruplets, leading to incorrect results.

solution_breakdown.intuition: The problem asks for four numbers that sum to a target, indicating a need for a Two Pointers approach to find pairs that sum to a remaining target. The presence of a target sum and the need to find multiple elements that add up to it suggests using Two Pointers. This approach allows for efficient searching of pairs within the array.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: The Two Pointers pattern is connected to this problem as it enables the efficient searching of pairs within the array that sum to a target, which is crucial for finding four numbers that sum to the target. The sorted array is essential for applying the Two Pointers technique.

steps.title: Sort the Array
steps.detail: Sort the input array in ascending order to prepare it for the Two Pointers technique.

steps.title: Fix Two Numbers
steps.detail: Fix two numbers in the array and calculate the remaining target sum for the other two numbers.

steps.title: Apply Two Pointers
steps.detail: Use the Two Pointers technique to find the remaining two numbers that sum to the target, starting from the next number after the fixed numbers and moving towards the end of the array.

steps.title: Skip Duplicates
steps.detail: Skip duplicate quadruplets by checking if the current numbers are the same as the previous ones to avoid incorrect results.

steps.title: Store Results
steps.detail: Store the quadruplets that sum to the target in a result list.

edge_cases: Array with less than four elements
Target sum is greater than the sum of all elements in the array

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach has a higher time complexity of O(n^4) compared to the Two Pointers technique, making it less efficient for large inputs.

binary-tree-preorder-traversal.title: Binary Tree Preorder Traversal
binary-tree-preorder-traversal.description_html:
Given the root of a binary tree, return the preorder traversal of its nodes' values.

 

Example 1:

Input: root = [1,null,2,3]

Output: [1,2,3]

Explanation:

Example 2:

Input: root = [1,2,3,4,5,null,8,null,null,6,7,9]

Output: [1,2,4,5,6,7,3,8,9]

Explanation:

Example 3:

Input: root = []

Output: []

Example 4:

Input: root = [1]

Output: [1]

 

Constraints:

  • The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 100].
  • -100 <= Node.val <= 100

 

Follow up: Recursive solution is trivial, could you do it iteratively?

binary-tree-preorder-traversal.pattern_hint: The problem involves traversing a binary tree, and the word 'preorder' specifically indicates a Trees pattern where we visit the root before the children. The tree structure itself signals the use of a recursive or iterative tree traversal approach.
binary-tree-preorder-traversal.key_insight: The key insight is to understand the order of visitation in a preorder traversal: root, left subtree, right subtree. This can be achieved through recursion or iteration using a stack.
binary-tree-preorder-traversal.template_deviation: This problem does not significantly deviate from the standard tree traversal template, as it directly applies the preorder traversal algorithm.
binary-tree-preorder-traversal.common_mistake: A common mistake is forgetting to handle the case where a node is null, leading to runtime errors.

solution_breakdown.intuition: The problem involves traversing a binary tree, and the word 'preorder' specifically indicates a Trees pattern where we visit the root before the children. The tree structure itself signals the use of a recursive or iterative tree traversal approach. This approach allows for efficient visitation of all nodes in the tree.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: The Trees pattern is connected to this problem as it enables the efficient traversal of the binary tree, visiting the root before the children. The recursive or iterative approach is crucial for traversing the tree.

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach uses a stack to store nodes to be visited, which can be more memory-efficient than the recursive approach for very large trees.

online-stock-span.title: Online Stock Span
online-stock-span.description_html:
Design an algorithm that collects daily price quotes for some stock and returns the span of that stock's price for the current day.

The span of the stock's price in one day is the maximum number of consecutive days (starting from that day and going backward) for which the stock price was less than or equal to the price of that day.

  • For example, if the prices of the stock in the last four days is [7,2,1,2] and the price of the stock today is 2, then the span of today is 4 because starting from today, the price of the stock was less than or equal 2 for 4 consecutive days.
  • Also, if the prices of the stock in the last four days is [7,34,1,2] and the price of the stock today is 8, then the span of today is 3 because starting from today, the price of the stock was less than or equal 8 for 3 consecutive days.

Implement the StockSpanner class:

  • StockSpanner() Initializes the object of the class.
  • int next(int price) Returns the span of the stock's price given that today's price is price.

 

Example 1:

Input
["StockSpanner", "next", "next", "next", "next", "next", "next", "next"]
[[], [100], [80], [60], [70], [60], [75], [85]]
Output
[null, 1, 1, 1, 2, 1, 4, 6]

Explanation
StockSpanner stockSpanner = new StockSpanner();
stockSpanner.next(100); // return 1
stockSpanner.next(80);  // return 1
stockSpanner.next(60);  // return 1
stockSpanner.next(70);  // return 2
stockSpanner.next(60);  // return 1
stockSpanner.next(75);  // return 4, because the last 4 prices (including today's price of 75) were less than or equal to today's price.
stockSpanner.next(85);  // return 6

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= price <= 105
  • At most 104 calls will be made to next.

For example, if the prices of the stock in the last four days is [7,2,1,2] and the price of the stock today is 2 , then the span of today is 4 because starting from today, the price of the stock was less than or equal 2 for 4 consecutive days. Also, if the prices of the stock in the last four days is [7,34,1,2] and the price of the stock today is 8 , then the span of today is 3 because starting from today, the price of the stock was less than or equal 8 for 3 consecutive days. Implement the StockSpanner class: StockSpanner() Initializes the object of the class. int next(int price) Returns the span of the stock's price given that today's price is price . Example 1: Input ["StockSpanner", "next", "next", "next", "next", "next", "next", "next"] [[], [100], [80], [60], [70], [60], [75], [85]] Output [null, 1, 1, 1, 2, 1, 4, 6] Explanation StockSpanner stockSpanner = new StockSpanner(); stockSpanner.next(100); // return 1 stockSpanner.next(80); // return 1 stockSpanner.next(60); // return 1 stockSpanner.next(70); // return 2 stockSpanner.next(60); // return 1 stockSpanner.next(75); // return 4, because the last 4 prices (including today's price of 75) were less than or equal to today's price. stockSpanner.next(85); // return 6 Constraints: 1 <= price <= 105 At most 104 calls will be made to next .
online-stock-span.pattern_hint: The problem involves processing a stream of stock prices and maintaining a span of consecutive days where the price is less than or equal to the current price, indicating a Stack pattern. The need to efficiently add and remove elements based on a condition suggests using a stack.
online-stock-span.key_insight: The key insight is to use a stack to keep track of the indices of the prices, where the stack is updated based on whether the current price is greater than the price at the top of the stack.
online-stock-span.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard stack template by requiring the maintenance of a 'span' which involves calculating the difference between the current index and the index at the top of the stack plus one.
online-stock-span.common_mistake: A common mistake is not correctly updating the stack when a new price is encountered, leading to incorrect span calculations.

solution_breakdown.intuition: The problem involves processing a stream of stock prices and maintaining a span of consecutive days where the price is less than or equal to the current price, indicating a Stack pattern. The need to efficiently add and remove elements based on a condition suggests using a stack. This approach allows for efficient calculation of the span.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: The Stack pattern is connected to this problem as it enables the efficient maintenance of the span of consecutive days where the price is less than or equal to the current price. The stack is updated based on whether the current price is greater than the price at the top of the stack.

steps.title: Initialize the Stack
steps.detail: Initialize an empty stack to store the indices of the prices.

steps.title: Process Each Price
steps.detail: Process each price in the stream, updating the stack and calculating the span.

steps.title: Update the Stack
steps.detail: Update the stack by popping elements that have a price greater than or equal to the current price and pushing the current index.

steps.title: Calculate the Span
steps.detail: Calculate the span by finding the difference between the current index and the index at the top of the stack minus one.

steps.title: Handle Edge Cases
steps.detail: Handle edge cases such as an empty stack or a price that is greater than the price at the top of the stack.

edge_cases: Price is greater than the price at the top of the stack
Price is less than or equal to the price at the top of the stack

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach has a higher time complexity of O(n^2) compared to the Stack approach, making it less efficient for large inputs.

single-element-in-a-sorted-array.title: Single Element in a Sorted Array
single-element-in-a-sorted-array.description_html:
You are given a sorted array consisting of only integers where every element appears exactly twice, except for one element which appears exactly once.

Return the single element that appears only once.

Your solution must run in O(log n) time and O(1) space.

 

Example 1:

Input: nums = [1,1,2,3,3,4,4,8,8]
Output: 2

Example 2:

Input: nums = [3,3,7,7,10,11,11]
Output: 10

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= nums.length <= 105
  • 0 <= nums[i] <= 105

single-element-in-a-sorted-array.common_mistake: A common mistake is not correctly handling the case when the single element is at an even or odd index, leading to incorrect search range adjustments.

palindrome-linked-list.title: Palindrome Linked List
palindrome-linked-list.description_html:
Given the head of a singly linked list, return true if it is a palindrome or false otherwise.

 

Example 1:

Input: head = [1,2,2,1]
Output: true

Example 2:

Input: head = [1,2]
Output: false

 

Constraints:

  • The number of nodes in the list is in the range [1, 105].
  • 0 <= Node.val <= 9

 

Follow up: Could you do it in O(n) time and O(1) space?

palindrome-linked-list.pattern_hint: The problem mentions a linked list and asks to determine if it's a palindrome, indicating the use of a linked list pattern. The word 'palindrome' suggests a comparison of the list's elements from both ends.
palindrome-linked-list.key_insight: The core idea is to compare elements from the start and end of the list, moving towards the center. This can be achieved by reversing the second half of the list and comparing it with the first half.
palindrome-linked-list.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard linked list template by requiring the comparison of elements from both ends, rather than just traversing the list in one direction.
palindrome-linked-list.common_mistake: A common mistake is to try to reverse the entire list, which is unnecessary and inefficient.

solution_breakdown.intuition: The problem asks to determine if a linked list is a palindrome, which means comparing elements from both ends and moving towards the center. This can be achieved by reversing the second half of the list and comparing it with the first half. The key is to find the middle of the list and reverse the second half.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem connects to the linked list pattern, where we need to traverse and manipulate the list to solve the problem. The palindrome property suggests a comparison of elements from both ends, which is a common technique in linked list problems.

intersection-of-two-linked-lists.title: Intersection of Two Linked Lists
intersection-of-two-linked-lists.description_html:
Given the heads of two singly linked-lists headA and headB, return the node at which the two lists intersect. If the two linked lists have no intersection at all, return null.

For example, the following two linked lists begin to intersect at node c1:

The test cases are generated such that there are no cycles anywhere in the entire linked structure.

Note that the linked lists must retain their original structure after the function returns.

Custom Judge:

The inputs to the judge are given as follows (your program is not given these inputs):

  • intersectVal - The value of the node where the intersection occurs. This is 0 if there is no intersected node.
  • listA - The first linked list.
  • listB - The second linked list.
  • skipA - The number of nodes to skip ahead in listA (starting from the head) to get to the intersected node.
  • skipB - The number of nodes to skip ahead in listB (starting from the head) to get to the intersected node.

The judge will then create the linked structure based on these inputs and pass the two heads, headA and headB to your program. If you correctly return the intersected node, then your solution will be accepted.

 

Example 1:

Input: intersectVal = 8, listA = [4,1,8,4,5], listB = [5,6,1,8,4,5], skipA = 2, skipB = 3
Output: Intersected at '8'
Explanation: The intersected node's value is 8 (note that this must not be 0 if the two lists intersect).
From the head of A, it reads as [4,1,8,4,5]. From the head of B, it reads as [5,6,1,8,4,5]. There are 2 nodes before the intersected node in A; There are 3 nodes before the intersected node in B.
- Note that the intersected node's value is not 1 because the nodes with value 1 in A and B (2nd node in A and 3rd node in B) are different node references. In other words, they point to two different locations in memory, while the nodes with value 8 in A and B (3rd node in A and 4th node in B) point to the same location in memory.

Example 2:

Input: intersectVal = 2, listA = [1,9,1,2,4], listB = [3,2,4], skipA = 3, skipB = 1
Output: Intersected at '2'
Explanation: The intersected node's value is 2 (note that this must not be 0 if the two lists intersect).
From the head of A, it reads as [1,9,1,2,4]. From the head of B, it reads as [3,2,4]. There are 3 nodes before the intersected node in A; There are 1 node before the intersected node in B.

Example 3:

Input: intersectVal = 0, listA = [2,6,4], listB = [1,5], skipA = 3, skipB = 2
Output: No intersection
Explanation: From the head of A, it reads as [2,6,4]. From the head of B, it reads as [1,5]. Since the two lists do not intersect, intersectVal must be 0, while skipA and skipB can be arbitrary values.
Explanation: The two lists do not intersect, so return null.

 

Constraints:

  • The number of nodes of listA is in the m.
  • The number of nodes of listB is in the n.
  • 1 <= m, n <= 3 * 104
  • 1 <= Node.val <= 105
  • 0 <= skipA <= m
  • 0 <= skipB <= n
  • intersectVal is 0 if listA and listB do not intersect.
  • intersectVal == listA[skipA] == listB[skipB] if listA and listB intersect.

 

Follow up: Could you write a solution that runs in O(m + n) time and use only O(1) memory?

intersection-of-two-linked-lists.pattern_hint: The problem involves two linked lists and asks to find their intersection, indicating the use of a linked list pattern. The word 'intersection' suggests a meeting point between the two lists.
intersection-of-two-linked-lists.key_insight: The key idea is to calculate the lengths of both lists and then move the longer list's pointer forward by the difference in lengths, so that both pointers are the same distance from the end. Then, move both pointers one step at a time and check for intersection.
intersection-of-two-linked-lists.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard linked list template by requiring the calculation of list lengths and the synchronization of pointers.
intersection-of-two-linked-lists.common_mistake: A common mistake is to try to compare the lists element by element without considering their lengths.

solution_breakdown.intuition: The problem asks to find the intersection of two linked lists, which means finding the common node where the two lists meet. This can be achieved by calculating the lengths of both lists and then moving the longer list's pointer forward by the difference in lengths. The key is to find the difference in lengths and move the pointers accordingly.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem connects to the linked list pattern, where we need to traverse and compare the lists to find the intersection. The intersection property suggests a meeting point between the two lists, which is a common technique in linked list problems.

convert-sorted-array-to-binary-search-tr.title: Convert Sorted Array to Binary Search Tree
convert-sorted-array-to-binary-search-tr.description_html:
Given an integer array nums where the elements are sorted in ascending order, convert it to a height-balanced binary search tree.

 

Example 1:

Input: nums = [-10,-3,0,5,9]
Output: [0,-3,9,-10,null,5]
Explanation: [0,-10,5,null,-3,null,9] is also accepted:

Example 2:

Input: nums = [1,3]
Output: [3,1]
Explanation: [1,null,3] and [3,1] are both height-balanced BSTs.

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= nums.length <= 104
  • -104 <= nums[i] <= 104
  • nums is sorted in a strictly increasing order.

Constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 104 -104 <= nums[i] <= 104 nums is sorted in a strictly increasing order.

convert-sorted-array-to-binary-search-tr.key_insight: The core idea is to recursively select the middle element of the array as the root of the tree, and then construct the left and right subtrees from the elements before and after the middle element.

solution_breakdown.intuition: The problem asks to convert a sorted array into a binary search tree, which means creating a tree where the left subtree of a node contains only nodes with values less than the node's value and the right subtree contains only nodes with values greater than the node's value. This can be achieved by recursively selecting the middle element of the array as the root of the tree and constructing the left and right subtrees from the elements before and after the middle element.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem connects to the tree pattern, where we need to construct a tree from a sorted array. The binary search tree property suggests a natural ordering of the elements, which is a common technique in tree problems.

symmetric-tree.title: Symmetric Tree
symmetric-tree.description_html:
Given the root of a binary tree, check whether it is a mirror of itself (i.e., symmetric around its center).

 

Example 1:

Input: root = [1,2,2,3,4,4,3]
Output: true

Example 2:

Input: root = [1,2,2,null,3,null,3]
Output: false

 

Constraints:

  • The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [1, 1000].
  • -100 <= Node.val <= 100

 

Follow up: Could you solve it both recursively and iteratively?

populating-next-right-pointers-in-each-n.title: Populating Next Right Pointers In Each Node
populating-next-right-pointers-in-each-n.description_html:
You are given a perfect binary tree where all leaves are on the same level, and every parent has two children. The binary tree has the following definition:

struct Node {
  int val;
  Node *left;
  Node *right;
  Node *next;
}

Populate each next pointer to point to its next right node. If there is no next right node, the next pointer should be set to NULL.

Initially, all next pointers are set to NULL.

 

Example 1:

Input: root = [1,2,3,4,5,6,7]
Output: [1,#,2,3,#,4,5,6,7,#]
Explanation: Given the above perfect binary tree (Figure A), your function should populate each next pointer to point to its next right node, just like in Figure B. The serialized output is in level order as connected by the next pointers, with '#' signifying the end of each level.

Example 2:

Input: root = []
Output: []

 

Constraints:

  • The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 212 - 1].
  • -1000 <= Node.val <= 1000

 

Follow-up:

  • You may only use constant extra space.
  • The recursive approach is fine. You may assume implicit stack space does not count as extra space for this problem.

binary-tree-zigzag-level-order-traversal.title: Binary Tree Zigzag Level Order Traversal
binary-tree-zigzag-level-order-traversal.description_html:
Given the root of a binary tree, return the zigzag level order traversal of its nodes' values. (i.e., from left to right, then right to left for the next level and alternate between).

 

Example 1:

Input: root = [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]
Output: [[3],[20,9],[15,7]]

Example 2:

Input: root = [1]
Output: [[1]]

Example 3:

Input: root = []
Output: []

 

Constraints:

  • The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 2000].
  • -100 <= Node.val <= 100

rotate-list.title: Rotate List
rotate-list.description_html:
Given the head of a linked list, rotate the list to the right by k places.

 

Example 1:

Input: head = [1,2,3,4,5], k = 2
Output: [4,5,1,2,3]

Example 2:

Input: head = [0,1,2], k = 4
Output: [2,0,1]

 

Constraints:

  • The number of nodes in the list is in the range [0, 500].
  • -100 <= Node.val <= 100
  • 0 <= k <= 2 * 109

construct-binary-tree-from-inorder-and-p.title: Construct Binary Tree from Inorder and Postorder Traversal
construct-binary-tree-from-inorder-and-p.description_html:
Given two integer arrays inorder and postorder where inorder is the inorder traversal of a binary tree and postorder is the postorder traversal of the same tree, construct and return the binary tree.

 

Example 1:

Input: inorder = [9,3,15,20,7], postorder = [9,15,7,20,3]
Output: [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]

Example 2:

Input: inorder = [-1], postorder = [-1]
Output: [-1]

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= inorder.length <= 3000
  • postorder.length == inorder.length
  • -3000 <= inorder[i], postorder[i] <= 3000
  • inorder and postorder consist of unique values.
  • Each value of postorder also appears in inorder.
  • inorder is guaranteed to be the inorder traversal of the tree.
  • postorder is guaranteed to be the postorder traversal of the tree.

binary-search-tree-iterator.title: Binary Search Tree Iterator
binary-search-tree-iterator.description_html:
Implement the BSTIterator class that represents an iterator over the in-order traversal of a binary search tree (BST):

  • BSTIterator(TreeNode root) Initializes an object of the BSTIterator class. The root of the BST is given as part of the constructor. The pointer should be initialized to a non-existent number smaller than any element in the BST.
  • boolean hasNext() Returns true if there exists a number in the traversal to the right of the pointer, otherwise returns false.
  • int next() Moves the pointer to the right, then returns the number at the pointer.

Notice that by initializing the pointer to a non-existent smallest number, the first call to next() will return the smallest element in the BST.

You may assume that next() calls will always be valid. That is, there will be at least a next number in the in-order traversal when next() is called.

 

Example 1:

Input
["BSTIterator", "next", "next", "hasNext", "next", "hasNext", "next", "hasNext", "next", "hasNext"]
[[[7, 3, 15, null, null, 9, 20]], [], [], [], [], [], [], [], [], []]
Output
[null, 3, 7, true, 9, true, 15, true, 20, false]

Explanation
BSTIterator bSTIterator = new BSTIterator([7, 3, 15, null, null, 9, 20]);
bSTIterator.next();    // return 3
bSTIterator.next();    // return 7
bSTIterator.hasNext(); // return True
bSTIterator.next();    // return 9
bSTIterator.hasNext(); // return True
bSTIterator.next();    // return 15
bSTIterator.hasNext(); // return True
bSTIterator.next();    // return 20
bSTIterator.hasNext(); // return False

 

Constraints:

  • The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [1, 105].
  • 0 <= Node.val <= 106
  • At most 105 calls will be made to hasNext, and next.

 

Follow up:

  • Could you implement next() and hasNext() to run in average O(1) time and use O(h) memory, where h is the height of the tree?

minimum-path-sum.title: Minimum Path Sum
minimum-path-sum.description_html:
Given a m x n grid filled with non-negative numbers, find a path from top left to bottom right, which minimizes the sum of all numbers along its path.

Note: You can only move either down or right at any point in time.

 

Example 1:

Input: grid = [[1,3,1],[1,5,1],[4,2,1]]
Output: 7
Explanation: Because the path 1 → 3 → 1 → 1 → 1 minimizes the sum.

Example 2:

Input: grid = [[1,2,3],[4,5,6]]
Output: 12

 

Constraints:

  • m == grid.length
  • n == grid[i].length
  • 1 <= m, n <= 200
  • 0 <= grid[i][j] <= 200
minimum-path-sum.description_text: Given a m x n grid filled with non-negative numbers, find a path from top left to bottom right, which minimizes the sum of all numbers along its path. Note: You can only move either down or right at any point in time. Example 1: Input: grid = [[1,3,1],[1,5,1],[4,2,1]] Output: 7 Explanation: Because the path 1 → 3 → 1 → 1 → 1 minimizes the sum. Example 2: Input: grid = [[1,2,3],[4,5,6]] Output: 12 Constraints: m == grid.length n == grid[i].length 1 <= m, n <= 200 0 <= grid[i][j] <= 200
minimum-path-sum.pattern_hint: The problem involves a grid and a path sum, indicating a 2D DP pattern. The requirement to find the minimum path sum suggests a need to use dynamic programming to build up the solution.
minimum-path-sum.key_insight: The core idea is to use a 2D array to store the minimum path sum to each cell, and fill it in row by row or column by column using the minimum path sum to the adjacent cells.
minimum-path-sum.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard 2D DP template by requiring the use of a 2D array to store the minimum path sum to each cell.
minimum-path-sum.common_mistake: A common mistake is incorrectly initializing the 2D array or filling it in incorrectly, resulting in an incorrect minimum path sum.

solution_breakdown.intuition: The problem requires finding the minimum path sum from the top-left to the bottom-right of a grid, which can be achieved by using dynamic programming to build up the solution. The key insight is to use a 2D array to store the minimum path sum to each cell and fill it in row by row or column by column using the minimum path sum to the adjacent cells.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem connects to the 2D DP pattern, specifically finding the minimum path sum, and requires using a 2D array to store the minimum path sum to each cell.

steps.title: Initialize 2D Array
steps.detail: Initialize a 2D array to store the minimum path sum to each cell.

steps.title: Fill in First Row and Column
steps.detail: Fill in the first row and column of the 2D array using the minimum path sum to the adjacent cells.

steps.title: Fill in Rest of 2D Array
steps.detail: Fill in the rest of the 2D array using the minimum path sum to the adjacent cells.

steps.title: Return Minimum Path Sum
steps.detail: Return the minimum path sum stored in the bottom-right cell of the 2D array.

steps.title: Handle Edge Cases
steps.detail: Handle edge cases such as an empty grid or a grid with one cell.

edge_cases: Grid with multiple rows and columns

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach is less efficient than the dynamic programming approach, as it requires more function calls and may lead to stack overflow for large grids.

lfu-cache.title: LFU Cache
lfu-cache.description_html:
Design and implement a data structure for a Least Frequently Used (LFU) cache.

Implement the LFUCache class:

  • LFUCache(int capacity) Initializes the object with the capacity of the data structure.
  • int get(int key) Gets the value of the key if the key exists in the cache. Otherwise, returns -1.
  • void put(int key, int value) Update the value of the key if present, or inserts the key if not already present. When the cache reaches its capacity, it should invalidate and remove the least frequently used key before inserting a new item. For this problem, when there is a tie (i.e., two or more keys with the same frequency), the least recently used key would be invalidated.

To determine the least frequently used key, a use counter is maintained for each key in the cache. The key with the smallest use counter is the least frequently used key.

When a key is first inserted into the cache, its use counter is set to 1 (due to the put operation). The use counter for a key in the cache is incremented either a get or put operation is called on it.

The functions get and put must each run in O(1) average time complexity.

 

Example 1:

Input
["LFUCache", "put", "put", "get", "put", "get", "get", "put", "get", "get", "get"]
[[2], [1, 1], [2, 2], [1], [3, 3], [2], [3], [4, 4], [1], [3], [4]]
Output
[null, null, null, 1, null, -1, 3, null, -1, 3, 4]

Explanation
// cnt(x) = the use counter for key x
// cache=[] will show the last used order for tiebreakers (leftmost element is  most recent)
LFUCache lfu = new LFUCache(2);
lfu.put(1, 1);   // cache=[1,_], cnt(1)=1
lfu.put(2, 2);   // cache=[2,1], cnt(2)=1, cnt(1)=1
lfu.get(1);      // return 1
                 // cache=[1,2], cnt(2)=1, cnt(1)=2
lfu.put(3, 3);   // 2 is the LFU key because cnt(2)=1 is the smallest, invalidate 2.
                 // cache=[3,1], cnt(3)=1, cnt(1)=2
lfu.get(2);      // return -1 (not found)
lfu.get(3);      // return 3
                 // cache=[3,1], cnt(3)=2, cnt(1)=2
lfu.put(4, 4);   // Both 1 and 3 have the same cnt, but 1 is LRU, invalidate 1.
                 // cache=[4,3], cnt(4)=1, cnt(3)=2
lfu.get(1);      // return -1 (not found)
lfu.get(3);      // return 3
                 // cache=[3,4], cnt(4)=1, cnt(3)=3
lfu.get(4);      // return 4
                 // cache=[4,3], cnt(4)=2, cnt(3)=3

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= capacity <= 104
  • 0 <= key <= 105
  • 0 <= value <= 109
  • At most 2 * 105 calls will be made to get and put.
