Each of the nine 3 x 3 sub-boxes of the grid must contain the digits 1-9 without repetition. Note: A Sudoku board (partially filled) could be valid but is not necessarily solvable. Only the filled cells need to be validated according to the mentioned rules. Example 1: Input: board = [["5","3",".",".","7",".",".",".","."] ,["6",".",".","1","9","5",".",".","."] ,[".","9","8",".",".",".",".","6","."] ,["8",".",".",".","6",".",".",".","3"] ,["4",".",".","8",".","3",".",".","1"] ,["7",".",".",".","2",".",".",".","6"] ,[".","6",".",".",".",".","2","8","."] ,[".",".",".","4","1","9",".",".","5"] ,[".",".",".",".","8",".",".","7","9"]] Output: true Example 2: Input: board = [["8","3",".",".","7",".",".",".","."] ,["6",".",".","1","9","5",".",".","."] ,[".","9","8",".",".",".",".","6","."] ,["8",".",".",".","6",".",".",".","3"] ,["4",".",".","8",".","3",".",".","1"] ,["7",".",".",".","2",".",".",".","6"] ,[".","6",".",".",".",".","2","8","."] ,[".",".",".","4","1","9",".",".","5"] ,[".",".",".",".","8",".",".","7","9"]] Output: false Explanation: Same as Example 1, except with the 5 in the top left corner being modified to 8. Since there are two 8's in the top left 3x3 sub-box, it is invalid. Constraints: board.length == 9 board[i].length == 9 board[i][j] is a digit 1-9 or '.' .
valid-sudoku.pattern_hint: The problem involves checking if a Sudoku board is valid, which hints at using arrays and hashing. The fact that we need to check each row, column, and 3x3 box suggests a systematic approach.
valid-sudoku.key_insight: The key insight is to realize that we can use hash sets to keep track of the numbers seen in each row, column, and 3x3 box, and check for duplicates.
valid-sudoku.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard arrays and hashing template in that we need to use multiple hash sets to keep track of the different regions of the Sudoku board.
valid-sudoku.common_mistake: A common mistake is to forget to check all three regions (rows, columns, and 3x3 boxes) for duplicates.

solution_breakdown.intuition: The core idea is to efficiently check for duplicates within each row, column, and 3x3 subgrid. Using hash sets allows for O(1) average time complexity for checking if a number has already been seen in a particular region.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem directly utilizes the Arrays & Hashing pattern by employing hash sets to track seen elements, enabling quick duplicate detection across different constraints (rows, columns, boxes).

steps.title: Initialize Data Structures
steps.detail: Create three arrays of hash sets: one for rows, one for columns, and one for 3x3 subgrids.

steps.title: Iterate Through Board
steps.detail: Traverse each cell of the 9x9 Sudoku board.

steps.title: Check for Duplicates
steps.detail: For each non-empty cell, check if the number already exists in the corresponding row, column, and subgrid hash sets.

steps.title: Add to Sets
steps.detail: If no duplicate is found, add the number to the respective row, column, and subgrid hash sets.

steps.title: Handle Invalid Input
steps.detail: If a duplicate is found at any point, immediately return false.

steps.title: Return True
steps.detail: If the entire board is traversed without finding any duplicates, return true.

edge_cases: An empty board (though constraints usually prevent this).
A board filled with only '.' characters.
A board with invalid characters other than '.' and '1'-'9'.
A board where a row, column, or box is valid but another is not.
A board that is almost valid but has a single duplicate.
A board that is completely filled and valid.

alternatives.tradeoff: Checking each row, column, and box using nested loops without hash sets would lead to a much higher time complexity, likely O(n^3) or worse, compared to the O(n^2) of the hash set approach.

alternatives.approach: Using 2D Arrays instead of Hash Sets
alternatives.tradeoff: Using boolean 2D arrays (e.g., `boolean[9][10]`) to mark seen numbers is also efficient and might have slightly better constant factors than hash sets, but conceptually it's very similar to the hash set approach.

largest-number.title: Largest Number
largest-number.description_html:
Given a list of non-negative integers nums, arrange them such that they form the largest number and return it.

Since the result may be very large, so you need to return a string instead of an integer.

 

Example 1:

Input: nums = [10,2]
Output: "210"

Example 2:

Input: nums = [3,30,34,5,9]
Output: "9534330"

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= nums.length <= 100
  • 0 <= nums[i] <= 109
largest-number.description_text: Given a list of non-negative integers nums , arrange them such that they form the largest number and return it. Since the result may be very large, so you need to return a string instead of an integer. Example 1: Input: nums = [10,2] Output: "210" Example 2: Input: nums = [3,30,34,5,9] Output: "9534330" Constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 100 0 <= nums[i] <= 109
largest-number.pattern_hint: The problem involves finding the largest number that can be formed by concatenating a list of numbers, which hints at using arrays and hashing. The fact that we need to compare numbers based on their concatenated values suggests a custom sorting approach.
largest-number.key_insight: The key insight is to realize that we can use a custom sorting approach to compare numbers based on their concatenated values, and then concatenate the sorted numbers to form the largest number.
largest-number.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard arrays and hashing template in that we need to use a custom sorting approach instead of a standard sorting algorithm.
largest-number.common_mistake: A common mistake is to forget to handle the edge case where the input list is empty or contains only zeros.

solution_breakdown.intuition: To form the largest number, we need to arrange the given numbers such that concatenating them in that order results in the lexicographically largest string. This requires a custom comparison logic that considers the combined effect of two numbers when placed next to each other.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem leverages the Arrays & Hashing pattern by treating numbers as strings and using a custom comparator within a sorting algorithm to arrange them based on a specific concatenation rule.

steps.title: Convert Numbers to Strings
steps.detail: Convert all integers in the input array to their string representations.

steps.title: Define Custom Comparator
steps.detail: Create a comparator that, for any two strings `a` and `b`, compares `b + a` with `a + b`. The order that results in a larger concatenated string is preferred.

steps.title: Sort the Strings
steps.detail: Sort the array of strings using the custom comparator defined in the previous step.

steps.title: Concatenate Sorted Strings
steps.detail: Join the sorted strings together to form the final result string.

steps.title: Handle Leading Zeros
steps.detail: If the resulting string starts with '0' and has more than one character, it means all numbers were zeros, so return '0'.

steps.title: Return Result
steps.detail: Return the concatenated string, which represents the largest possible number.

edge_cases: Input array contains a mix of zeros and other numbers.
Input array contains numbers with different lengths.

alternatives.tradeoff: Generating all permutations of the numbers and checking each concatenated string would be computationally infeasible due to the factorial time complexity (O(n! * n)), whereas the custom sort approach is O(n log n * k) where k is the average number of digits.

two-sum-ii-input-array-is-sorted.title: Two Sum II Input Array Is Sorted
two-sum-ii-input-array-is-sorted.description_html:
Given a 1-indexed array of integers numbers that is already sorted in non-decreasing order, find two numbers such that they add up to a specific target number. Let these two numbers be numbers[index1] and numbers[index2] where 1 <= index1 < index2 <= numbers.length.

Return the indices of the two numbers index1 and index2, each incremented by one, as an integer array [index1, index2] of length 2.

The tests are generated such that there is exactly one solution. You may not use the same element twice.

Your solution must use only constant extra space.

 

Example 1:

Input: numbers = [2,7,11,15], target = 9
Output: [1,2]
Explanation: The sum of 2 and 7 is 9. Therefore, index1 = 1, index2 = 2. We return [1, 2].

Example 2:

Input: numbers = [2,3,4], target = 6
Output: [1,3]
Explanation: The sum of 2 and 4 is 6. Therefore index1 = 1, index2 = 3. We return [1, 3].

Example 3:

Input: numbers = [-1,0], target = -1
Output: [1,2]
Explanation: The sum of -1 and 0 is -1. Therefore index1 = 1, index2 = 2. We return [1, 2].

 

Constraints:

  • 2 <= numbers.length <= 3 * 104
  • -1000 <= numbers[i] <= 1000
  • numbers is sorted in non-decreasing order.
  • -1000 <= target <= 1000
  • The tests are generated such that there is exactly one solution.
two-sum-ii-input-array-is-sorted.description_text: Given a 1-indexed array of integers numbers that is already sorted in non-decreasing order, find two numbers such that they add up to a specific target number. Let these two numbers be numbers[index1] and numbers[index2] where 1 <= index1 < index2 <= numbers.length . Return the indices of the two numbers index1 and index2 , each incremented by one, as an integer array [index1, index2] of length 2. The tests are generated such that there is exactly one solution. You may not use the same element twice. Your solution must use only constant extra space. Example 1: Input: numbers = [2,7,11,15], target = 9 Output: [1,2] Explanation: The sum of 2 and 7 is 9. Therefore, index1 = 1, index2 = 2. We return [1, 2]. Example 2: Input: numbers = [2,3,4], target = 6 Output: [1,3] Explanation: The sum of 2 and 4 is 6. Therefore index1 = 1, index2 = 3. We return [1, 3]. Example 3: Input: numbers = [-1,0], target = -1 Output: [1,2] Explanation: The sum of -1 and 0 is -1. Therefore index1 = 1, index2 = 2. We return [1, 2]. Constraints: 2 <= numbers.length <= 3 * 104 -1000 <= numbers[i] <= 1000 numbers is sorted in non-decreasing order. -1000 <= target <= 1000 The tests are generated such that there is exactly one solution.
two-sum-ii-input-array-is-sorted.sub_pattern_name: Two Pointers Moving in Opposite Directions
two-sum-ii-input-array-is-sorted.pattern_hint: The problem statement mentions that the input array is sorted, which immediately suggests using a Two Pointers approach to find the two sum. The fact that the array is sorted allows for a more efficient solution than a brute force approach.
two-sum-ii-input-array-is-sorted.key_insight: The key insight is to use two pointers, one starting from the beginning of the array and one from the end, and move them towards each other based on the sum of the values at the pointers. This approach allows for a time complexity of O(n).
two-sum-ii-input-array-is-sorted.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard Two Pointers template in that the pointers are moving towards each other, rather than both moving in the same direction.
two-sum-ii-input-array-is-sorted.common_mistake: A common mistake is to not handle the case where the two pointers meet, which can happen if the two sum values are adjacent in the array.

solution_breakdown.intuition: Since the input array is sorted, we can efficiently find the pair of numbers that sum up to the target by using two pointers. One pointer starts at the beginning and the other at the end, moving inwards based on whether the current sum is too small, too large, or exactly the target.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem is a classic application of the Two Pointers pattern, specifically tailored for sorted arrays, allowing for a linear time solution by intelligently narrowing down the search space.

steps.title: Initialize Pointers
steps.detail: Initialize a left pointer `l` to the start of the array (index 0) and a right pointer `r` to the end of the array (index `n-1`).

steps.title: Iterate While Pointers Don't Cross
steps.detail: Continue the process as long as the left pointer `l` is less than the right pointer `r`.

steps.title: Calculate Current Sum
steps.detail: Calculate the sum of the elements at the current left and right pointers: `currentSum = numbers[l] + numbers[r]`.

steps.title: Compare Sum with Target
steps.detail: If `currentSum` equals the `target`, we have found the pair; return their indices (l+1 and r+1).

steps.title: Adjust Pointers
steps.detail: If `currentSum` is less than `target`, increment the left pointer `l` to increase the sum. If `currentSum` is greater than `target`, decrement the right pointer `r` to decrease the sum.

steps.title: Handle No Solution
steps.detail: If the loop finishes without finding a pair, it implies no solution exists (though the problem guarantees one).

edge_cases: The two numbers that sum to the target are adjacent.
The target requires the smallest and largest numbers in the array.
The target requires two numbers that are identical (if duplicates are allowed and present).
The array contains only two elements.
The target is very large or very small.

alternatives.tradeoff: A hash map approach (similar to the original Two Sum problem) would work but doesn't take advantage of the sorted nature of the array, resulting in O(n) time complexity and O(n) space complexity, whereas the two-pointer approach achieves O(n) time and O(1) space.
Checking every possible pair using nested loops would have a time complexity of O(n^2), which is significantly less efficient than the O(n) two-pointer solution.

evaluate-reverse-polish-notation.title: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation
evaluate-reverse-polish-notation.description_html:
You are given an array of strings tokens that represents an arithmetic expression in a Reverse Polish Notation.

Evaluate the expression. Return an integer that represents the value of the expression.

Note that:

  • The valid operators are '+', '-', '*', and '/'.
  • Each operand may be an integer or another expression.
  • The division between two integers always truncates toward zero.
  • There will not be any division by zero.
  • The input represents a valid arithmetic expression in a reverse polish notation.
  • The answer and all the intermediate calculations can be represented in a 32-bit integer.

 

Example 1:

Input: tokens = ["2","1","+","3","*"]
Output: 9
Explanation: ((2 + 1) * 3) = 9

Example 2:

Input: tokens = ["4","13","5","/","+"]
Output: 6
Explanation: (4 + (13 / 5)) = 6

Example 3:

Input: tokens = ["10","6","9","3","+","-11","*","/","*","17","+","5","+"]
Output: 22
Explanation: ((10 * (6 / ((9 + 3) * -11))) + 17) + 5
= ((10 * (6 / (12 * -11))) + 17) + 5
= ((10 * (6 / -132)) + 17) + 5
= ((10 * 0) + 17) + 5
= (0 + 17) + 5
= 17 + 5
= 22

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= tokens.length <= 104
  • tokens[i] is either an operator: "+", "-", "*", or "/", or an integer in the range [-200, 200].
evaluate-reverse-polish-notation.description_text: You are given an array of strings tokens that represents an arithmetic expression in a Reverse Polish Notation. Evaluate the expression. Return an integer that represents the value of the expression. Note that: The valid operators are '+' , '-' , '*' , and '/' . Each operand may be an integer or another expression. The division between two integers always truncates toward zero. There will not be any division by zero. The input represents a valid arithmetic expression in a reverse polish notation. The answer and all the intermediate calculations can be represented in a 32-bit integer. Example 1: Input: tokens = ["2","1","+","3","*"] Output: 9 Explanation: ((2 + 1) * 3) = 9 Example 2: Input: tokens = ["4","13","5","/","+"] Output: 6 Explanation: (4 + (13 / 5)) = 6 Example 3: Input: tokens = ["10","6","9","3","+","-11","*","/","*","17","+","5","+"] Output: 22 Explanation: ((10 * (6 / ((9 + 3) * -11))) + 17) + 5 = ((10 * (6 / (12 * -11))) + 17) + 5 = ((10 * (6 / -132)) + 17) + 5 = ((10 * 0) + 17) + 5 = (0 + 17) + 5 = 17 + 5 = 22 Constraints: 1 <= tokens.length <= 104 tokens[i] is either an operator: "+" , "-" , "*" , or "/" , or an integer in the range [-200, 200] .
evaluate-reverse-polish-notation.pattern_hint: The problem statement mentions that the input is in Reverse Polish Notation, which is a notation where operators follow their operands, suggesting the use of a Stack to evaluate the expression. The fact that the input is a list of tokens, where each token is either an operator or an operand, also suggests using a Stack.
evaluate-reverse-polish-notation.key_insight: The key insight is to use a Stack to store the operands and then pop them off the Stack when an operator is encountered, applying the operator to the popped operands and pushing the result back onto the Stack.
evaluate-reverse-polish-notation.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard Stack template in that the Stack is used to store operands, rather than just to keep track of function calls or recursion.
evaluate-reverse-polish-notation.common_mistake: A common mistake is to not handle the case where there are not enough operands on the Stack for an operator, which can happen if the input expression is invalid.

solution_breakdown.intuition: Reverse Polish Notation (RPN) naturally lends itself to a stack-based evaluation. Operands are pushed onto the stack, and when an operator is encountered, the required operands are popped, the operation is performed, and the result is pushed back.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem is a direct application of the Stack pattern. The LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) nature of a stack perfectly models the way operands are processed and results are stored in RPN.

steps.title: Initialize Stack
steps.detail: Create an empty stack to store operands (integers).

steps.title: Iterate Through Tokens
steps.detail: Process each token in the input array one by one.

steps.title: Handle Operands
steps.detail: If the token is a number, convert it to an integer and push it onto the stack.

steps.title: Handle Operators
steps.detail: If the token is an operator ('+', '-', '*', '/'), pop the top two operands from the stack.

steps.title: Perform Operation and Push Result
steps.detail: Perform the operation using the popped operands (paying attention to order for subtraction and division) and push the result back onto the stack.

steps.title: Return Final Result
steps.detail: After processing all tokens, the final result will be the only element left on the stack; pop and return it.

edge_cases: Invalid tokens in the input array.
Not enough operands for an operator.
Too many operands left on the stack at the end (malformed expression).
Input array contains only a single number.
Handling negative numbers as operands or results.

alternatives.tradeoff: A recursive approach could be devised, but it would likely be more complex to implement and manage the state compared to the straightforward iterative stack-based solution. It might also lead to stack overflow issues for deep expressions.

alternatives.approach: Two Stacks (one for operands, one for operators)
alternatives.tradeoff: While possible, using two stacks is unnecessarily complex for RPN evaluation. A single stack suffices because the structure of RPN dictates when operands are available for operations.

find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.title: Find Minimum In Rotated Sorted Array
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.description_html:
Suppose an array of length n sorted in ascending order is rotated between 1 and n times. For example, the array nums = [0,1,2,4,5,6,7] might become:

  • [4,5,6,7,0,1,2] if it was rotated 4 times.
  • [0,1,2,4,5,6,7] if it was rotated 7 times.

Notice that rotating an array [a[0], a[1], a[2], ..., a[n-1]] 1 time results in the array [a[n-1], a[0], a[1], a[2], ..., a[n-2]].

Given the sorted rotated array nums of unique elements, return the minimum element of this array.

You must write an algorithm that runs in O(log n) time.

 

Example 1:

Input: nums = [3,4,5,1,2]
Output: 1
Explanation: The original array was [1,2,3,4,5] rotated 3 times.

Example 2:

Input: nums = [4,5,6,7,0,1,2]
Output: 0
Explanation: The original array was [0,1,2,4,5,6,7] and it was rotated 4 times.

Example 3:

Input: nums = [11,13,15,17]
Output: 11
Explanation: The original array was [11,13,15,17] and it was rotated 4 times.

 

Constraints:

  • n == nums.length
  • 1 <= n <= 5000
  • -5000 <= nums[i] <= 5000
  • All the integers of nums are unique.
  • nums is sorted and rotated between 1 and n times.
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.description_text: Suppose an array of length n sorted in ascending order is rotated between 1 and n times. For example, the array nums = [0,1,2,4,5,6,7] might become: [4,5,6,7,0,1,2] if it was rotated 4 times. [0,1,2,4,5,6,7] if it was rotated 7 times. Notice that rotating an array [a[0], a[1], a[2], ..., a[n-1]] 1 time results in the array [a[n-1], a[0], a[1], a[2], ..., a[n-2]] . Given the sorted rotated array nums of unique elements, return the minimum element of this array. You must write an algorithm that runs in O(log n) time . Example 1: Input: nums = [3,4,5,1,2] Output: 1 Explanation: The original array was [1,2,3,4,5] rotated 3 times. Example 2: Input: nums = [4,5,6,7,0,1,2] Output: 0 Explanation: The original array was [0,1,2,4,5,6,7] and it was rotated 4 times. Example 3: Input: nums = [11,13,15,17] Output: 11 Explanation: The original array was [11,13,15,17] and it was rotated 4 times. Constraints: n == nums.length 1 <= n <= 5000 -5000 <= nums[i] <= 5000 All the integers of nums are unique. nums is sorted and rotated between 1 and n times.
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.pattern_hint: The problem statement mentions that the input array is sorted, but rotated, which suggests using a Binary Search approach to find the minimum value. The fact that the array is rotated, but still sorted, allows for a more efficient solution than a brute force approach.
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.key_insight: The key insight is to use a modified Binary Search approach, where the search space is reduced by half at each step, but the direction of the search is determined by the comparison of the middle element with the rightmost element.
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard Binary Search template in that the search space is not necessarily reduced by half at each step, but rather the direction of the search is determined by the comparison of the middle element with the rightmost element.
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.common_mistake: A common mistake is to not handle the case where the minimum value is at the beginning of the array, which can happen if the array is not rotated.

solution_breakdown.intuition: The array is sorted but rotated, meaning it consists of two sorted subarrays. We can use a modified binary search to efficiently find the minimum element by comparing the middle element with the rightmost element to determine which half contains the pivot (the minimum value).
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem is a prime example of applying the Binary Search pattern to a variation of a sorted array. The key is adapting the standard binary search conditions to handle the rotation point.

steps.title: Initialize Pointers
steps.detail: Set left pointer `l` to 0 and right pointer `r` to the last index of the array.

steps.title: Binary Search Loop
steps.detail: Continue the search as long as `l < r`.

steps.title: Calculate Midpoint
steps.detail: Calculate the middle index `mid = l + (r - l) / 2` to avoid potential integer overflow.

steps.title: Compare Mid with Right
steps.detail: Compare `nums[mid]` with `nums[r]`. If `nums[mid] < nums[r]`, the minimum must be in the left half (including `mid`), so set `r = mid`.

steps.title: Adjust Search Space
steps.detail: If `nums[mid] > nums[r]`, the minimum must be in the right half (excluding `mid`), so set `l = mid + 1`. If `nums[mid] == nums[r]`, we can safely discard the rightmost element by setting `r--` to handle duplicates.

steps.title: Return Minimum
steps.detail: When the loop terminates (`l == r`), `nums[l]` (or `nums[r]`) will be the minimum element.

edge_cases: The array is not rotated (already sorted).
The array has only one element.
The minimum element is the first element.
The minimum element is the last element.

alternatives.tradeoff: Iterating through the array linearly and keeping track of the minimum element found so far has a time complexity of O(n), which is less efficient than the O(log n) binary search approach.

subtree-of-another-tree.title: Subtree of Another Tree
subtree-of-another-tree.description_html:
Given the roots of two binary trees root and subRoot, return true if there is a subtree of root with the same structure and node values of subRoot and false otherwise.

A subtree of a binary tree tree is a tree that consists of a node in tree and all of this node's descendants. The tree tree could also be considered as a subtree of itself.

 

Example 1:

Input: root = [3,4,5,1,2], subRoot = [4,1,2]
Output: true

Example 2:

Input: root = [3,4,5,1,2,null,null,null,null,0], subRoot = [4,1,2]
Output: false

 

Constraints:

  • The number of nodes in the root tree is in the range [1, 2000].
  • The number of nodes in the subRoot tree is in the range [1, 1000].
  • -104 <= root.val <= 104
  • -104 <= subRoot.val <= 104
subtree-of-another-tree.description_text: Given the roots of two binary trees root and subRoot , return true if there is a subtree of root with the same structure and node values of subRoot and false otherwise. A subtree of a binary tree tree is a tree that consists of a node in tree and all of this node's descendants. The tree tree could also be considered as a subtree of itself. Example 1: Input: root = [3,4,5,1,2], subRoot = [4,1,2] Output: true Example 2: Input: root = [3,4,5,1,2,null,null,null,null,0], subRoot = [4,1,2] Output: false Constraints: The number of nodes in the root tree is in the range [1, 2000] . The number of nodes in the subRoot tree is in the range [1, 1000] . -104 <= root.val <= 104 -104 <= subRoot.val <= 104
subtree-of-another-tree.pattern_hint: The problem statement mentions that we need to check if a tree is a subtree of another tree, which suggests using a recursive approach to traverse the trees and compare the nodes. The fact that the trees are not necessarily balanced also suggests using a recursive approach.
subtree-of-another-tree.key_insight: The key insight is to use a recursive approach to traverse the trees and compare the nodes, and to use a helper function to check if a tree is a subtree of another tree.
subtree-of-another-tree.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard tree traversal template in that we need to compare the nodes of two trees, rather than just traversing a single tree.
subtree-of-another-tree.common_mistake: A common mistake is to not handle the case where the subtree is not found in the main tree, which can happen if the subtree is not a subtree of the main tree.

solution_breakdown.intuition: To determine if tree `t` is a subtree of tree `s`, we need to check two conditions: either `s` itself is identical to `t`, or `t` is a subtree of `s`'s left child, or `t` is a subtree of `s`'s right child. This suggests a recursive approach.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem involves traversing tree structures, making it a natural fit for recursion and tree-based algorithms. The pattern is essentially Tree Traversal combined with Tree Comparison.

steps.title: Base Cases for Main Function
steps.detail: If the main tree `s` is null, return false (unless `t` is also null, which is handled by the helper). If the subtree `t` is null, return true (an empty tree is a subtree of any tree).

steps.title: Check for Identical Trees
steps.detail: Implement a helper function `isSameTree(s, t)` that checks if two trees are structurally identical and have the same node values. Call this function with the current nodes of `s` and `t`.

steps.title: Recursive Calls
steps.detail: If `isSameTree(s, t)` returns true, then `t` is a subtree of `s`, so return true.

steps.title: Recurse on Children
steps.detail: If the trees are not identical at the current node, recursively call the main function `isSubtree(s.left, t)` and `isSubtree(s.right, t)`. Return true if either of these calls returns true.

steps.title: Handle No Match
steps.detail: If none of the above conditions are met after traversing `s`, return false.

steps.title: Helper Function Logic
steps.detail: The `isSameTree` helper function should also use recursion: base cases for null nodes, check current node values, and recursively call for left and right children.

edge_cases: The subtree `t` is identical to the main tree `s`.
The subtree `t` is found in the left child of `s`.
The subtree `t` is found in the right child of `s`.
The subtree `t` does not exist in `s`.

alternatives.tradeoff: Serialize both trees `s` and `t` into strings (e.g., using pre-order traversal with null markers). Then, check if the serialized string of `t` is a substring of the serialized string of `s`. This can be simpler to code but might be less efficient due to string operations and potentially larger memory usage.

design-add-and-search-words-data-structu.title: Design Add And Search Words Data Structure
design-add-and-search-words-data-structu.description_html:
Design a data structure that supports adding new words and finding if a string matches any previously added string.

Implement the WordDictionary class:

  • WordDictionary() Initializes the object.
  • void addWord(word) Adds word to the data structure, it can be matched later.
  • bool search(word) Returns true if there is any string in the data structure that matches word or false otherwise. word may contain dots '.' where dots can be matched with any letter.

 

Example:

Input
["WordDictionary","addWord","addWord","addWord","search","search","search","search"]
[[],["bad"],["dad"],["mad"],["pad"],["bad"],[".ad"],["b.."]]
Output
[null,null,null,null,false,true,true,true]

Explanation
WordDictionary wordDictionary = new WordDictionary();
wordDictionary.addWord("bad");
wordDictionary.addWord("dad");
wordDictionary.addWord("mad");
wordDictionary.search("pad"); // return False
wordDictionary.search("bad"); // return True
wordDictionary.search(".ad"); // return True
wordDictionary.search("b.."); // return True

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= word.length <= 25
  • word in addWord consists of lowercase English letters.
  • word in search consist of '.' or lowercase English letters.
  • There will be at most 2 dots in word for search queries.
  • At most 104 calls will be made to addWord and search.
design-add-and-search-words-data-structu.description_text: Design a data structure that supports adding new words and finding if a string matches any previously added string. Implement the WordDictionary class: WordDictionary() Initializes the object. void addWord(word) Adds word to the data structure, it can be matched later. bool search(word) Returns true if there is any string in the data structure that matches word or false otherwise. word may contain dots '.' where dots can be matched with any letter. Example: Input ["WordDictionary","addWord","addWord","addWord","search","search","search","search"] [[],["bad"],["dad"],["mad"],["pad"],["bad"],[".ad"],["b.."]] Output [null,null,null,null,false,true,true,true] Explanation WordDictionary wordDictionary = new WordDictionary(); wordDictionary.addWord("bad"); wordDictionary.addWord("dad"); wordDictionary.addWord("mad"); wordDictionary.search("pad"); // return False wordDictionary.search("bad"); // return True wordDictionary.search(".ad"); // return True wordDictionary.search("b.."); // return True Constraints: 1 <= word.length <= 25 word in addWord consists of lowercase English letters. word in search consist of '.' or lowercase English letters. There will be at most 2 dots in word for search queries. At most 104 calls will be made to addWord and search .
design-add-and-search-words-data-structu.pattern_hint: The problem statement mentions that we need to design a data structure to store words and search for words, which suggests using a Trie data structure. The fact that we need to search for words with wildcards also suggests using a Trie.
design-add-and-search-words-data-structu.key_insight: The key insight is to use a Trie data structure to store the words, and to use a recursive approach to search for words with wildcards.
design-add-and-search-words-data-structu.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard Trie template in that we need to handle wildcards in the search query, which requires a recursive approach to search for all possible matches.
design-add-and-search-words-data-structu.common_mistake: A common mistake is to not handle the case where the search query contains multiple wildcards, which can happen if the query is complex.

solution_breakdown.intuition: A Trie (prefix tree) is ideal for this problem because it efficiently stores words based on their prefixes. Searching for words, especially with wildcards, can be done by traversing the Trie, exploring different paths when a wildcard is encountered.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem directly utilizes the Trie data structure pattern. The Trie's structure allows for efficient prefix-based searching and insertion, which is crucial for handling word storage and wildcard searches.

steps.title: Define TrieNode Structure
steps.detail: Create a `TrieNode` class containing an array (or map) of children nodes (e.g., size 26 for lowercase English letters) and a boolean flag `isEndOfWord`.

steps.title: Initialize Trie
steps.detail: Create a root `TrieNode` for the main Trie structure.

steps.title: Implement `addWord`
steps.detail: Iterate through the characters of the word to be added. For each character, traverse or create the corresponding child node in the Trie. Mark the `isEndOfWord` flag of the final node as true.

steps.title: Implement `search` (Recursive Helper)
steps.detail: Create a recursive helper function `searchHelper(node, word, index)` that takes the current node, the word, and the current index in the word.

steps.title: Handle Wildcards and Characters
steps.detail: Inside the helper: if the character is '.', recursively call `searchHelper` for all possible children. If the character is a letter, move to the corresponding child node. Check `isEndOfWord` at the end of the word.

steps.title: Implement `search` (Main)
steps.detail: The main `search` function calls the helper starting from the root node and index 0.

edge_cases: Word search with multiple consecutive wildcards.
Word search where a wildcard matches the end of a word.
Adding the same word multiple times.

alternatives.tradeoff: Storing all words in a hash set allows for O(L) average time complexity for exact word search (where L is word length), but it doesn't efficiently handle wildcard searches. Implementing wildcard search with a hash set would likely require iterating through all stored words, leading to a much worse time complexity (potentially O(N*L) where N is the number of words).

house-robber.title: House Robber
house-robber.description_html:
You are a professional robber planning to rob houses along a street. Each house has a certain amount of money stashed, the only constraint stopping you from robbing each of them is that adjacent houses have security systems connected and it will automatically contact the police if two adjacent houses were broken into on the same night.

Given an integer array nums representing the amount of money of each house, return the maximum amount of money you can rob tonight without alerting the police.

 

Example 1:

Input: nums = [1,2,3,1]
Output: 4
Explanation: Rob house 1 (money = 1) and then rob house 3 (money = 3).
Total amount you can rob = 1 + 3 = 4.

Example 2:

Input: nums = [2,7,9,3,1]
Output: 12
Explanation: Rob house 1 (money = 2), rob house 3 (money = 9) and rob house 5 (money = 1).
Total amount you can rob = 2 + 9 + 1 = 12.

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= nums.length <= 100
  • 0 <= nums[i] <= 400
house-robber.description_text: You are a professional robber planning to rob houses along a street. Each house has a certain amount of money stashed, the only constraint stopping you from robbing each of them is that adjacent houses have security systems connected and it will automatically contact the police if two adjacent houses were broken into on the same night. Given an integer array nums representing the amount of money of each house, return the maximum amount of money you can rob tonight without alerting the police. Example 1: Input: nums = [1,2,3,1] Output: 4 Explanation: Rob house 1 (money = 1) and then rob house 3 (money = 3). Total amount you can rob = 1 + 3 = 4. Example 2: Input: nums = [2,7,9,3,1] Output: 12 Explanation: Rob house 1 (money = 2), rob house 3 (money = 9) and rob house 5 (money = 1). Total amount you can rob = 2 + 9 + 1 = 12. Constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 100 0 <= nums[i] <= 400
house-robber.pattern_hint: The problem involves maximizing a sum by making choices that depend on previous decisions, which suggests using dynamic programming. The 1-D array of houses indicates a sequential decision-making process.
house-robber.key_insight: The key insight is to realize that the decision to rob a house depends only on the decision made for the previous house, allowing for a simple recursive relationship. This relationship can be expressed as choosing the maximum between including the current house and excluding it.
house-robber.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard 1-D DP template by having a direct dependency only on the immediately preceding element, simplifying the transition function.
house-robber.common_mistake: A common mistake is to overlook the dependency between houses and try to solve it using a greedy approach without considering the impact of future decisions.

solution_breakdown.intuition: The decision to rob a house depends on whether the previous house was robbed. If we rob the current house, we cannot rob the previous one. If we don't rob the current house, we can take the maximum amount robbed from the previous house. This overlapping subproblem structure suggests dynamic programming.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem is a classic example of the 1-D Dynamic Programming pattern. The optimal solution for robbing `i` houses can be derived from the optimal solutions for robbing `i-1` and `i-2` houses.

steps.title: Define DP State
steps.detail: Let `dp[i]` be the maximum amount of money that can be robbed from the first `i` houses.

steps.title: Base Cases
steps.detail: Initialize `dp[0] = 0` (no houses, no money). If there's at least one house, `dp[1] = nums[0]` (rob the first house).

steps.title: Recurrence Relation
steps.detail:
For `i` from 2 to `n` (where `n` is the number of houses), the maximum amount is the greater of: 
1. Robbing house `i`: `nums[i-1] + dp[i-2]` (cannot rob house `i-1`).
2. Not robbing house `i`: `dp[i-1]`.

steps.title: Iteration
steps.detail: Iterate through the houses from the third house onwards, applying the recurrence relation to fill the `dp` array.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.detail: The maximum amount that can be robbed from all `n` houses is `dp[n]`.

steps.title: Space Optimization (Optional)
steps.detail: Notice that `dp[i]` only depends on `dp[i-1]` and `dp[i-2]`. We can optimize space by using only two variables to store the previous two maximum amounts instead of a full DP array.

edge_cases: Input array with only one house.
Large house values that might cause overflow if not handled carefully (though standard integer types are usually sufficient).
The optimal strategy involves skipping multiple houses.

alternatives.tradeoff: A top-down recursive approach with memoization can also solve this problem. It follows the same recurrence relation but uses a memoization table (similar to the DP array) to store results of subproblems. This has the same time complexity O(n) but might incur slightly more overhead due to recursive function calls and potentially use more space if the recursion depth is large.

A simple greedy approach (e.g., always robbing the house with the most money) will not work because it doesn't consider the constraint that adjacent houses cannot be robbed. This can lead to suboptimal solutions.

house-robber-ii.title: House Robber II
house-robber-ii.description_html:
You are a professional robber planning to rob houses along a street. Each house has a certain amount of money stashed. All houses at this place are arranged in a circle. That means the first house is the neighbor of the last one. Meanwhile, adjacent houses have a security system connected, and it will automatically contact the police if two adjacent houses were broken into on the same night.

Given an integer array nums representing the amount of money of each house, return the maximum amount of money you can rob tonight without alerting the police.

 

Example 1:

Input: nums = [2,3,2]
Output: 3
Explanation: You cannot rob house 1 (money = 2) and then rob house 3 (money = 2), because they are adjacent houses.

Example 2:

Input: nums = [1,2,3,1]
Output: 4
Explanation: Rob house 1 (money = 1) and then rob house 3 (money = 3).
Total amount you can rob = 1 + 3 = 4.

Example 3:

Input: nums = [1,2,3]
Output: 3

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= nums.length <= 100
  • 0 <= nums[i] <= 1000
house-robber-ii.description_text: You are a professional robber planning to rob houses along a street. Each house has a certain amount of money stashed. All houses at this place are arranged in a circle. That means the first house is the neighbor of the last one. Meanwhile, adjacent houses have a security system connected, and it will automatically contact the police if two adjacent houses were broken into on the same night. Given an integer array nums representing the amount of money of each house, return the maximum amount of money you can rob tonight without alerting the police. Example 1: Input: nums = [2,3,2] Output: 3 Explanation: You cannot rob house 1 (money = 2) and then rob house 3 (money = 2), because they are adjacent houses. Example 2: Input: nums = [1,2,3,1] Output: 4 Explanation: Rob house 1 (money = 1) and then rob house 3 (money = 3). Total amount you can rob = 1 + 3 = 4. Example 3: Input: nums = [1,2,3] Output: 3 Constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 100 0 <= nums[i] <= 1000
house-robber-ii.pattern_hint: Similar to the first house robber problem, this involves maximizing a sum in a sequence, but the circular arrangement of houses introduces a twist. The problem statement's mention of a circular array hints at the need to adjust the standard 1-D DP approach.
house-robber-ii.key_insight: The core idea is to recognize that the circular nature of the houses means the first and last houses are adjacent, requiring a modification to the standard approach to handle this edge case. This can be solved by considering two separate cases: one where the first house is robbed and one where it is not.
house-robber-ii.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard 1-D DP template by requiring consideration of the circular boundary condition, which affects how the base cases are defined.
house-robber-ii.common_mistake: A common mistake is failing to account for the circular dependency between the first and last houses, leading to incorrect handling of the boundary conditions.

solution_breakdown.intuition: Since the houses are in a circle, the first and last houses cannot be robbed together. This means we can solve the problem by considering two independent linear house robber problems: one excluding the last house and one excluding the first house. The maximum of these two results will be the answer.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem is a variation of the 1D DP pattern, specifically the House Robber problem. The circular arrangement requires a modification to the standard linear DP by breaking it into two linear subproblems.

steps.title: Handle Base Cases
steps.detail: If there's only one house, return its value; if there are two, return the maximum of the two.

steps.title: Define DP State
steps.detail: Let dp[i] be the maximum amount of money that can be robbed from houses 0 to i.

steps.title: Formulate Recurrence Relation
steps.detail: The recurrence is dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]), representing the choice to either skip house i or rob house i.

steps.title: Solve for First Subproblem
steps.detail: Calculate the maximum amount robbing houses from index 0 to n-2 (excluding the last house).

steps.title: Solve for Second Subproblem
steps.detail: Calculate the maximum amount robbing houses from index 1 to n-1 (excluding the first house).

steps.title: Return Maximum
steps.detail: The final answer is the maximum of the results from the two subproblems.

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach involves exploring all possible combinations of robbing houses, leading to exponential time complexity and is highly inefficient.

single-number.title: Single Number
single-number.description_html:
Given a non-empty array of integers nums, every element appears twice except for one. Find that single one.

You must implement a solution with a linear runtime complexity and use only constant extra space.

 

Example 1:

Input: nums = [2,2,1]

Output: 1

Example 2:

Input: nums = [4,1,2,1,2]

Output: 4

Example 3:

Input: nums = [1]

Output: 1

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= nums.length <= 3 * 104
  • -3 * 104 <= nums[i] <= 3 * 104
  • Each element in the array appears twice except for one element which appears only once.
single-number.description_text: Given a non-empty array of integers nums , every element appears twice except for one. Find that single one. You must implement a solution with a linear runtime complexity and use only constant extra space. Example 1: Input: nums = [2,2,1] Output: 1 Example 2: Input: nums = [4,1,2,1,2] Output: 4 Example 3: Input: nums = [1] Output: 1 Constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 3 * 104 -3 * 104 <= nums[i] <= 3 * 104 Each element in the array appears twice except for one element which appears only once.
single-number.pattern_hint: The problem statement's mention of finding a single number in an array where every other number appears twice hints at using a bitwise operation. The uniqueness of the single number suggests that XOR could be used to eliminate the pairs.
single-number.key_insight: The key insight is to recognize that XORing all numbers in the array will eliminate the numbers that appear twice, leaving only the single number. This is because XOR of a number with itself is 0, and XOR of a number with 0 is the number itself.
single-number.template_deviation: This problem deviates from more complex bit manipulation problems by requiring only a simple, iterative XOR operation without needing to consider bit positions or masks.
single-number.common_mistake: A common mistake is to overlook the properties of XOR and try to solve the problem using sorting or hashing, which are less efficient.

solution_breakdown.intuition: The XOR operation has the property that a number XORed with itself results in 0, and a number XORed with 0 results in the number itself. By XORing all elements in the array, pairs of identical numbers will cancel each other out (become 0), leaving only the single, unique number.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem is a classic application of the Bit Manipulation pattern, specifically leveraging the properties of the XOR operator.

steps.title: Initialize Result
steps.detail: Initialize a variable, say 'result', to 0.

steps.title: Iterate Through Array
steps.detail: Loop through each number in the input array.

steps.title: Apply XOR Operation
steps.detail: In each iteration, XOR the current number with the 'result' variable and update 'result'.

steps.title: Return Result
steps.detail: After iterating through all numbers, the value of 'result' will be the single number that appears only once.

edge_cases: Array where the single number is at the beginning or end

alternatives.tradeoff: Using a hash set involves adding elements and removing them if they appear twice, which has O(n) time complexity but requires O(n) space complexity, unlike the O(1) space complexity of the XOR approach.
Sorting the array and then iterating to find the single element works but has a time complexity of O(n log n), which is less efficient than the O(n) time complexity of the XOR approach.

reverse-integer.title: Reverse Integer
reverse-integer.description_html:
Given a signed 32-bit integer x, return x with its digits reversed. If reversing x causes the value to go outside the signed 32-bit integer range [-231, 231 - 1], then return 0.

Assume the environment does not allow you to store 64-bit integers (signed or unsigned).

 

Example 1:

Input: x = 123
Output: 321

Example 2:

Input: x = -123
Output: -321

Example 3:

Input: x = 120
Output: 21

 

Constraints:

  • -231 <= x <= 231 - 1
reverse-integer.description_text: Given a signed 32-bit integer x , return x with its digits reversed. If reversing x causes the value to go outside the signed 32-bit integer range [-231, 231 - 1] , then return 0 . Assume the environment does not allow you to store 64-bit integers (signed or unsigned). Example 1: Input: x = 123 Output: 321 Example 2: Input: x = -123 Output: -321 Example 3: Input: x = 120 Output: 21 Constraints: -231 <= x <= 231 - 1
reverse-integer.pattern_hint: The problem involves manipulating the digits of an integer, which suggests using bit manipulation or arithmetic operations to reverse the number. The need to handle overflow indicates careful consideration of the integer's sign and magnitude.
reverse-integer.key_insight: The core idea is to repeatedly take the last digit of the number (using the modulo operator), add it to the reversed number (after shifting its current digits to the left), and then remove the last digit from the original number. Special care must be taken to handle the sign and potential overflow.
reverse-integer.template_deviation: This problem deviates from standard bit manipulation problems by focusing on arithmetic operations and the specific challenge of handling integer overflow.
reverse-integer.common_mistake: A common mistake is to forget to check for overflow after reversing the integer, which can lead to incorrect results for large inputs.

solution_breakdown.intuition: To reverse an integer, we can extract digits from the original number one by one using the modulo operator and build the reversed number by multiplying the current reversed number by 10 and adding the extracted digit. We must also handle potential integer overflow.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem falls under the Bit Manipulation pattern, although it primarily uses arithmetic operations. The core idea involves digit manipulation and careful handling of integer limits, similar to problems that might involve bitwise checks for overflow.

steps.title: Initialize Reversed Integer
steps.detail: Initialize a variable 'reversedNum' to 0.

steps.title: Iterate While Number is Not Zero
steps.detail: Loop as long as the input number 'x' is not zero.

steps.title: Extract Last Digit
steps.detail: Get the last digit using the modulo operator: 'digit = x % 10'.

steps.title: Check for Overflow
steps.detail: Before updating 'reversedNum', check if multiplying it by 10 and adding 'digit' would cause an overflow (exceed Integer.MAX_VALUE or fall below Integer.MIN_VALUE).

steps.title: Build Reversed Integer
steps.detail: Update 'reversedNum': 'reversedNum = reversedNum * 10 + digit'.

steps.title: Remove Last Digit
steps.detail: Remove the last digit from 'x': 'x = x / 10'.

steps.title: Return Result
steps.detail: If overflow occurred, return 0; otherwise, return 'reversedNum'.

edge_cases: Input is a single digit number

alternatives.tradeoff: Converting the integer to a string, reversing the string, and converting it back to an integer is simpler to implement but less efficient due to the overhead of string operations and potential issues with handling the sign and overflow.

remove-element.title: Remove Element
remove-element.description_html:
Given an integer array nums and an integer val, remove all occurrences of val in nums in-place. The order of the elements may be changed. Then return the number of elements in nums which are not equal to val.

Consider the number of elements in nums which are not equal to val be k, to get accepted, you need to do the following things:

  • Change the array nums such that the first k elements of nums contain the elements which are not equal to val. The remaining elements of nums are not important as well as the size of nums.
  • Return k.

Custom Judge:

The judge will test your solution with the following code:

int[] nums = [...]; // Input array
int val = ...; // Value to remove
int[] expectedNums = [...]; // The expected answer with correct length.
                            // It is sorted with no values equaling val.

int k = removeElement(nums, val); // Calls your implementation

assert k == expectedNums.length;
sort(nums, 0, k); // Sort the first k elements of nums
for (int i = 0; i < actualLength; i++) {
    assert nums[i] == expectedNums[i];
}

If all assertions pass, then your solution will be accepted.

 

Example 1:

Input: nums = [3,2,2,3], val = 3
Output: 2, nums = [2,2,_,_]
Explanation: Your function should return k = 2, with the first two elements of nums being 2.
It does not matter what you leave beyond the returned k (hence they are underscores).

Example 2:

Input: nums = [0,1,2,2,3,0,4,2], val = 2
Output: 5, nums = [0,1,4,0,3,_,_,_]
Explanation: Your function should return k = 5, with the first five elements of nums containing 0, 0, 1, 3, and 4.
Note that the five elements can be returned in any order.
It does not matter what you leave beyond the returned k (hence they are underscores).

 

Constraints:

  • 0 <= nums.length <= 100
  • 0 <= nums[i] <= 50
  • 0 <= val <= 100
remove-element.description_text: Given an integer array nums and an integer val , remove all occurrences of val in nums in-place. The order of the elements may be changed. Then return the number of elements in nums which are not equal to val . Consider the number of elements in nums which are not equal to val be k , to get accepted, you need to do the following things: Change the array nums such that the first k elements of nums contain the elements which are not equal to val . The remaining elements of nums are not important as well as the size of nums . Return k . Custom Judge: The judge will test your solution with the following code: int[] nums = [...]; // Input array int val = ...; // Value to remove int[] expectedNums = [...]; // The expected answer with correct length. // It is sorted with no values equaling val. int k = removeElement(nums, val); // Calls your implementation assert k == expectedNums.length; sort(nums, 0, k); // Sort the first k elements of nums for (int i = 0; i < actualLength; i++) { assert nums[i] == expectedNums[i]; } If all assertions pass, then your solution will be accepted. Example 1: Input: nums = [3,2,2,3], val = 3 Output: 2, nums = [2,2,_,_] Explanation: Your function should return k = 2, with the first two elements of nums being 2. It does not matter what you leave beyond the returned k (hence they are underscores). Example 2: Input: nums = [0,1,2,2,3,0,4,2], val = 2 Output: 5, nums = [0,1,4,0,3,_,_,_] Explanation: Your function should return k = 5, with the first five elements of nums containing 0, 0, 1, 3, and 4. Note that the five elements can be returned in any order. It does not matter what you leave beyond the returned k (hence they are underscores). Constraints: 0 <= nums.length <= 100 0 <= nums[i] <= 50 0 <= val <= 100
remove-element.pattern_hint: The problem involves modifying an array in-place, which suggests using array manipulation techniques. The requirement to remove elements based on a condition hints at using a two-pointer approach.
remove-element.key_insight: The key insight is to use two pointers, one for reading and one for writing, to iterate through the array and only write elements that do not match the condition to be removed. This approach ensures that all elements not equal to the target value are moved to the front of the array.
remove-element.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard two-pointer technique by requiring in-place modification and a specific condition for element removal, which affects how the pointers are moved.
remove-element.common_mistake: A common mistake is to try to solve the problem by shifting all elements after the removed one, which is inefficient and not necessary with the two-pointer approach.

solution_breakdown.intuition: We can solve this efficiently by using a two-pointer approach. One pointer iterates through the array, and another pointer keeps track of the position where the next non-removed element should be placed. This allows us to overwrite elements to be removed with valid elements in a single pass.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem is a classic example of the Arrays & Hashing pattern, specifically utilizing the Two Pointers technique for in-place array modification.

steps.title: Initialize Write Pointer
steps.detail: Initialize a 'write_index' (or 'k') to 0, which will track the position for the next element to keep.

steps.title: Iterate Through Array
steps.detail: Use a 'read_index' (or 'i') to iterate through the entire input array 'nums' from beginning to end.

steps.title: Check Element Value
steps.detail: For each element 'nums[read_index]', check if it is equal to the value 'val' that needs to be removed.

steps.title: Keep Element
steps.detail: If 'nums[read_index]' is NOT equal to 'val', copy it to the position indicated by 'write_index': 'nums[write_index] = nums[read_index]'.

steps.title: Increment Write Pointer
steps.detail: If an element was kept (i.e., not equal to 'val'), increment 'write_index' by 1.

steps.title: Return New Length
steps.detail: After the loop finishes, 'write_index' will represent the number of elements remaining in the array (the new effective length). Return 'write_index'.

edge_cases: All elements need to be removed
No elements need to be removed
The element to remove is at the beginning/end
The array contains duplicates of the element to remove

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach involves creating a new array and copying only the desired elements, which is simpler conceptually but requires O(n) extra space and is not an in-place solution.
For each element to remove, swap it with the last element and decrease the effective array size. This is efficient (O(n) time, O(1) space) but changes the relative order of the remaining elements.

unique-email-addresses.title: Unique Email Addresses
unique-email-addresses.description_html:
Every valid email consists of a local name and a domain name, separated by the '@' sign. Besides lowercase letters, the email may contain one or more '.' or '+'.

  • For example, in "alice@leetcode.com", "alice" is the local name, and "leetcode.com" is the domain name.

If you add periods '.' between some characters in the local name part of an email address, mail sent there will be forwarded to the same address without dots in the local name. Note that this rule does not apply to domain names.

  • For example, "alice.z@leetcode.com" and "alicez@leetcode.com" forward to the same email address.

If you add a plus '+' in the local name, everything after the first plus sign will be ignored. This allows certain emails to be filtered. Note that this rule does not apply to domain names.

  • For example, "m.y+name@email.com" will be forwarded to "my@email.com".

It is possible to use both of these rules at the same time.

Given an array of strings emails where we send one email to each emails[i], return the number of different addresses that actually receive mails.

 

Example 1:

Input: emails = ["test.email+alex@leetcode.com","test.e.mail+bob.cathy@leetcode.com","testemail+david@lee.tcode.com"]
Output: 2
Explanation: "testemail@leetcode.com" and "testemail@lee.tcode.com" actually receive mails.

Example 2:

Input: emails = ["a@leetcode.com","b@leetcode.com","c@leetcode.com"]
Output: 3

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= emails.length <= 100
  • 1 <= emails[i].length <= 100
  • emails[i] consist of lowercase English letters, '+', '.' and '@'.
  • Each emails[i] contains exactly one '@' character.
  • All local and domain names are non-empty.
  • Local names do not start with a '+' character.
  • Domain names end with the ".com" suffix.
  • Domain names must contain at least one character before ".com" suffix.
