Constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 5 * 104 -5 * 104 <= nums[i] <= 5 * 104
sort-an-array.pattern_hint: The problem involves sorting an array, which can be solved using various sorting algorithms that utilize Arrays & Hashing for efficiency. The presence of an array and the need to sort it points towards using Arrays & Hashing.
sort-an-array.key_insight: The core idea is to choose an appropriate sorting algorithm (like quicksort, mergesort, or heapsort) that can efficiently sort the array, considering factors like time complexity, space complexity, and stability. This involves understanding the trade-offs between different algorithms and selecting the most suitable one.
sort-an-array.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard hashing template by requiring the implementation of a sorting algorithm, which may not necessarily involve hashing but can benefit from it in certain scenarios like counting sort or radix sort.
sort-an-array.common_mistake: A common mistake is choosing an inappropriate sorting algorithm for the given scenario, leading to inefficient or incorrect results.
sort-an-array.space_complexity: O(n) for some algorithms, O(1) for others

solution_breakdown.intuition: To sort an array efficiently, we need an algorithm that can achieve O(N log N) time complexity, which is the theoretical lower bound for comparison-based sorts. Divide and conquer algorithms like Merge Sort or Quick Sort are ideal for this.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem is a fundamental "Arrays & Hashing" problem, as sorting algorithms are core array manipulation techniques that often involve partitioning, comparing, and rearranging elements within the array structure.

steps.title: Base Case
steps.detail: If the array (or sub-array) has zero or one element, it is already sorted and can be returned.

steps.title: Divide
steps.detail: Split the current array into two halves, typically at the midpoint.

steps.title: Conquer
steps.detail: Recursively call the sort function on both the left and right halves to sort them independently.

steps.title: Merge
steps.detail: Combine the two sorted halves back into a single sorted array by comparing elements from each half and placing them in order into a temporary array.

steps.title: Copy Back
steps.detail: Copy the elements from the temporary merged array back into the original array's segment.

edge_cases: An array with a single element `[5]`.
An array with all identical elements `[3, 3, 3, 3]`.
An array already sorted in ascending order `[1, 2, 3, 4]`.
An array sorted in descending order `[4, 3, 2, 1]`.

alternatives.tradeoff: Generally faster in practice due to better cache performance and lower constant factors, but has a worst-case O(N^2) time complexity (though rare with good pivot selection) and is not stable.
Offers O(N log N) time complexity with O(1) auxiliary space (in-place), but typically has worse cache performance than Merge Sort or Quick Sort and is not stable.

alternatives.approach: Insertion Sort / Selection Sort / Bubble Sort
alternatives.tradeoff: Simpler to implement but have O(N^2) time complexity, making them inefficient for large datasets.

brick-wall.title: Brick Wall
brick-wall.description_html:
There is a rectangular brick wall in front of you with n rows of bricks. The ith row has some number of bricks each of the same height (i.e., one unit) but they can be of different widths. The total width of each row is the same.

Draw a vertical line from the top to the bottom and cross the least bricks. If your line goes through the edge of a brick, then the brick is not considered as crossed. You cannot draw a line just along one of the two vertical edges of the wall, in which case the line will obviously cross no bricks.

Given the 2D array wall that contains the information about the wall, return the minimum number of crossed bricks after drawing such a vertical line.

 

Example 1:

Input: wall = [[1,2,2,1],[3,1,2],[1,3,2],[2,4],[3,1,2],[1,3,1,1]]
Output: 2

Example 2:

Input: wall = [[1],[1],[1]]
Output: 3

 

Constraints:

  • n == wall.length
  • 1 <= n <= 104
  • 1 <= wall[i].length <= 104
  • 1 <= sum(wall[i].length) <= 2 * 104
  • sum(wall[i]) is the same for each row i.
  • 1 <= wall[i][j] <= 231 - 1
brick-wall.description_text: There is a rectangular brick wall in front of you with n rows of bricks. The ith row has some number of bricks each of the same height (i.e., one unit) but they can be of different widths. The total width of each row is the same. Draw a vertical line from the top to the bottom and cross the least bricks. If your line goes through the edge of a brick, then the brick is not considered as crossed. You cannot draw a line just along one of the two vertical edges of the wall, in which case the line will obviously cross no bricks. Given the 2D array wall that contains the information about the wall, return the minimum number of crossed bricks after drawing such a vertical line. Example 1: Input: wall = [[1,2,2,1],[3,1,2],[1,3,2],[2,4],[3,1,2],[1,3,1,1]] Output: 2 Example 2: Input: wall = [[1],[1],[1]] Output: 3 Constraints: n == wall.length 1 <= n <= 104 1 <= wall[i].length <= 104 1 <= sum(wall[i].length) <= 2 * 104 sum(wall[i]) is the same for each row i . 1 <= wall[i][j] <= 231 - 1
brick-wall.pattern_hint: The problem involves a list of edges and a width, hinting at using a hash map to store the frequency of edge positions. The goal is to find the minimum number of cuts, which suggests using a hashing approach to count the edges at each position.
brick-wall.key_insight: The core idea is to use a hash map to store the frequency of edge positions and find the maximum frequency, which represents the minimum number of cuts. This is because the maximum frequency of edge positions corresponds to the minimum number of cuts needed to avoid a straight line.
brick-wall.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard hashing template by requiring the use of a hash map to store the frequency of edge positions, rather than just storing unique elements.
brick-wall.common_mistake: A common mistake is to forget to consider the case where the width of the wall is not a factor of the total width, which can lead to incorrect calculations.

solution_breakdown.intuition: The problem asks for the minimum number of bricks to cut to draw a vertical line from top to bottom without crossing any brick. This is equivalent to finding a vertical line position where the maximum number of brick edges align. If we draw a line at such a position, we only cut the bricks that do not have an edge there, thus minimizing cuts.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem is a classic application of Arrays & Hashing, as it involves counting the frequencies of specific values (edge positions) to identify the most common alignment, which directly leads to the optimal solution.

steps.title: Initialize Frequency Map
steps.detail: Create a hash map to store the frequency of each possible vertical edge position within the wall.

steps.title: Iterate Through Rows
steps.detail: Process each row of bricks in the wall one by one to identify all internal edge positions.

steps.title: Calculate Edge Positions and Frequencies
steps.detail: For each row, calculate the cumulative sum of brick widths to find the exact vertical position of each internal edge, and increment its count in the hash map.

steps.title: Find Maximum Edge Frequency
steps.detail: After processing all rows, iterate through the hash map to find the maximum frequency recorded for any single edge position.

steps.title: Calculate Minimum Cuts
steps.detail: The minimum number of bricks to cut is the total number of rows in the wall minus this maximum edge frequency, as these are the rows where a line can pass through an existing gap.

edge_cases: Wall where all rows have only one brick (no internal edges).
Wall where all bricks in a row have the same width.
Wall where no two internal edges align across different rows.
Wall where all internal edges align at a single position.
Wall where all bricks are of width 1.

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach would involve iterating through every possible vertical line position from 1 to the total wall width, counting cuts for each, which is much slower as it considers positions that might not correspond to any brick edge.

maximum-product-of-the-length-of-two-pal.title: Maximum Product of The Length of Two Palindromic Subsequences
maximum-product-of-the-length-of-two-pal.description_html:
Given a string s, find two disjoint palindromic subsequences of s such that the product of their lengths is maximized. The two subsequences are disjoint if they do not both pick a character at the same index.

Return the maximum possible product of the lengths of the two palindromic subsequences.

A subsequence is a string that can be derived from another string by deleting some or no characters without changing the order of the remaining characters. A string is palindromic if it reads the same forward and backward.

 

Example 1:

Input: s = "leetcodecom"
Output: 9
Explanation: An optimal solution is to choose "ete" for the 1st subsequence and "cdc" for the 2nd subsequence.
The product of their lengths is: 3 * 3 = 9.

Example 2:

Input: s = "bb"
Output: 1
Explanation: An optimal solution is to choose "b" (the first character) for the 1st subsequence and "b" (the second character) for the 2nd subsequence.
The product of their lengths is: 1 * 1 = 1.

Example 3:

Input: s = "accbcaxxcxx"
Output: 25
Explanation: An optimal solution is to choose "accca" for the 1st subsequence and "xxcxx" for the 2nd subsequence.
The product of their lengths is: 5 * 5 = 25.

 

Constraints:

  • 2 <= s.length <= 12
  • s consists of lowercase English letters only.
maximum-product-of-the-length-of-two-pal.description_text: Given a string s , find two disjoint palindromic subsequences of s such that the product of their lengths is maximized. The two subsequences are disjoint if they do not both pick a character at the same index. Return the maximum possible product of the lengths of the two palindromic subsequences. A subsequence is a string that can be derived from another string by deleting some or no characters without changing the order of the remaining characters. A string is palindromic if it reads the same forward and backward. Example 1: Input: s = "leetcodecom" Output: 9 Explanation: An optimal solution is to choose "ete" for the 1st subsequence and "cdc" for the 2nd subsequence. The product of their lengths is: 3 * 3 = 9. Example 2: Input: s = "bb" Output: 1 Explanation: An optimal solution is to choose "b" (the first character) for the 1st subsequence and "b" (the second character) for the 2nd subsequence. The product of their lengths is: 1 * 1 = 1. Example 3: Input: s = "accbcaxxcxx" Output: 25 Explanation: An optimal solution is to choose "accca" for the 1st subsequence and "xxcxx" for the 2nd subsequence. The product of their lengths is: 5 * 5 = 25. Constraints: 2 <= s.length <= 12 s consists of lowercase English letters only.
maximum-product-of-the-length-of-two-pal.pattern_hint: The problem involves finding palindromic subsequences, which suggests using a hashing approach to store the subsequences and their lengths. The goal is to find the maximum product of two palindromic subsequences, which hints at using a dynamic programming approach.
maximum-product-of-the-length-of-two-pal.key_insight: The core idea is to use dynamic programming to store the lengths of palindromic subsequences and then find the maximum product of two palindromic subsequences. This involves using a 2D array to store the lengths of palindromic subsequences and then iterating over the array to find the maximum product.
maximum-product-of-the-length-of-two-pal.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard hashing template by requiring the use of dynamic programming to store the lengths of palindromic subsequences, rather than just storing unique elements.
maximum-product-of-the-length-of-two-pal.common_mistake: A common mistake is to forget to consider the case where the two palindromic subsequences are not disjoint, which can lead to incorrect calculations.

solution_breakdown.intuition: The problem requires finding two disjoint palindromic subsequences with the maximum product of their lengths. Since subsequences are formed by picking characters, and disjointness means no shared characters, bitmasking is an efficient way to represent all possible subsequences and check for disjointness. We can precompute the longest palindromic subsequence (LPS) length for every possible character subset.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem effectively combines bitmasking to represent subsets of characters with dynamic programming (or memoization) to precompute the LPS length for each subset, which is a common technique in problems involving subsets and optimal substructure.

steps.title: Precompute LPS for All Subsequences
steps.detail: For every possible subsequence of the input string (represented by a bitmask), calculate and store the length of its longest palindromic subsequence (LPS) using memoization.

steps.title: Define LPS Calculation Function
steps.detail: Implement a recursive helper function `calculateLPS(mask)` that finds the LPS length for the characters indicated by the mask, using the standard LPS logic (matching first/last characters or taking max of subproblems).

steps.title: Initialize Maximum Product
steps.detail: Set a variable `maxProduct` to 0 (or 1 if string length can be 0 or 1, but constraints usually prevent this) to track the highest product found.

steps.title: Iterate Through All Mask Pairs
steps.detail: Loop through all possible pairs of bitmasks, `mask1` and `mask2`, representing two potential subsequences.

steps.title: Check for Disjointness
steps.detail: For each pair, check if `(mask1 & mask2) == 0`, which signifies that the two subsequences are disjoint (share no common characters).

steps.title: Update Maximum Product
steps.detail: If `mask1` and `mask2` are disjoint, multiply their precomputed LPS lengths (`memo.get(mask1) * memo.get(mask2)`) and update `maxProduct` if the current product is greater.

edge_cases: String with no palindromic subsequences of length greater than 1 (e.g., "abcde").
String where the optimal subsequences are very short.
String where the optimal subsequences are long and utilize most characters.
String where multiple pairs of disjoint subsequences yield the same maximum product.

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach would involve generating all 2^N subsequences, checking each for palindrome property, and then iterating through all pairs of palindromic subsequences to check for disjointness and calculate products, leading to extremely high time complexity.

A standard 2D DP approach for finding the LPS of a single string (e.g., `dp[i][j]`) does not directly extend to finding *two disjoint* palindromic subsequences without significant modifications, as it doesn't naturally handle the 'disjoint' character constraint across two separate subsequences.

find-all-anagrams-in-a-string.title: Find All Anagrams in a String
find-all-anagrams-in-a-string.description_html:
Given two strings s and p, return an array of all the start indices of p's anagrams in s. You may return the answer in any order.

 

Example 1:

Input: s = "cbaebabacd", p = "abc"
Output: [0,6]
Explanation:
The substring with start index = 0 is "cba", which is an anagram of "abc".
The substring with start index = 6 is "bac", which is an anagram of "abc".

Example 2:

Input: s = "abab", p = "ab"
Output: [0,1,2]
Explanation:
The substring with start index = 0 is "ab", which is an anagram of "ab".
The substring with start index = 1 is "ba", which is an anagram of "ab".
The substring with start index = 2 is "ab", which is an anagram of "ab".

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= s.length, p.length <= 3 * 104
  • s and p consist of lowercase English letters.
find-all-anagrams-in-a-string.description_text: Given two strings s and p , return an array of all the start indices of p 's anagrams in s . You may return the answer in any order. Example 1: Input: s = "cbaebabacd", p = "abc" Output: [0,6] Explanation: The substring with start index = 0 is "cba", which is an anagram of "abc". The substring with start index = 6 is "bac", which is an anagram of "abc". Example 2: Input: s = "abab", p = "ab" Output: [0,1,2] Explanation: The substring with start index = 0 is "ab", which is an anagram of "ab". The substring with start index = 1 is "ba", which is an anagram of "ab". The substring with start index = 2 is "ab", which is an anagram of "ab". Constraints: 1 <= s.length, p.length <= 3 * 104 s and p consist of lowercase English letters.
find-all-anagrams-in-a-string.pattern_hint: The problem involves finding anagrams in a string, which suggests using a hashing approach to store the frequency of characters in the anagram and the string. The goal is to find all starting indices of anagrams, which hints at using a sliding window approach.
find-all-anagrams-in-a-string.key_insight: The core idea is to use a sliding window approach to compare the frequency of characters in the anagram and the string, and then use a hash map to store the frequency of characters. This involves using a hash map to store the frequency of characters in the anagram and the string, and then iterating over the string to find the anagrams.
find-all-anagrams-in-a-string.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard hashing template by requiring the use of a sliding window approach to compare the frequency of characters, rather than just storing unique elements.
find-all-anagrams-in-a-string.common_mistake: A common mistake is to forget to consider the case where the anagram is not a contiguous substring, which can lead to incorrect calculations.

solution_breakdown.intuition: Since we are looking for anagrams, which are permutations of a string, the character counts must match. We can use a sliding window to efficiently check character counts within substrings of the target string that are the same length as the pattern string.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem is a classic application of the sliding window technique combined with hashing (frequency maps) to efficiently compare character counts.

steps.title: Initialize Frequency Maps
steps.detail: Create a frequency map for the pattern string and an empty frequency map for the current window in the text string.

steps.title: Populate Initial Window
steps.detail: Populate the window frequency map with the characters from the first 'p.length()' characters of the text string.

steps.title: Compare Initial Window
steps.detail: Compare the pattern frequency map with the initial window frequency map; if they match, add index 0 to the result list.

steps.title: Slide the Window
steps.detail: Iterate through the text string, sliding the window one character at a time: decrement the count of the character leaving the window and increment the count of the character entering the window.

steps.title: Compare Current Window
steps.detail: After each slide, compare the updated window frequency map with the pattern frequency map; if they match, add the current window's start index to the result list.

steps.title: Return Results
steps.detail: Return the list of all starting indices where anagrams were found.

edge_cases: The text string 's' is shorter than the pattern string 'p'.
The pattern string 'p' contains characters not present in 's'.
The text string 's' contains characters not present in 'p'.
All characters in 's' are the same, and 'p' is a substring of 's'.

alternatives.tradeoff: Extremely inefficient due to the factorial time complexity of generating permutations, making it infeasible for larger inputs.
Less efficient than the sliding window with frequency maps because sorting each substring takes O(k log k) time, where k is the length of the pattern.

find-the-index-of-the-first-occurrence-i.title: Find The Index of The First Occurrence in a String
find-the-index-of-the-first-occurrence-i.description_html:
Given two strings needle and haystack, return the index of the first occurrence of needle in haystack, or -1 if needle is not part of haystack.

 

Example 1:

Input: haystack = "sadbutsad", needle = "sad"
Output: 0
Explanation: "sad" occurs at index 0 and 6.
The first occurrence is at index 0, so we return 0.

Example 2:

Input: haystack = "leetcode", needle = "leeto"
Output: -1
Explanation: "leeto" did not occur in "leetcode", so we return -1.

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= haystack.length, needle.length <= 104
  • haystack and needle consist of only lowercase English characters.
find-the-index-of-the-first-occurrence-i.description_text: Given two strings needle and haystack , return the index of the first occurrence of needle in haystack , or -1 if needle is not part of haystack . Example 1: Input: haystack = "sadbutsad", needle = "sad" Output: 0 Explanation: "sad" occurs at index 0 and 6. The first occurrence is at index 0, so we return 0. Example 2: Input: haystack = "leetcode", needle = "leeto" Output: -1 Explanation: "leeto" did not occur in "leetcode", so we return -1. Constraints: 1 <= haystack.length, needle.length <= 104 haystack and needle consist of only lowercase English characters.
find-the-index-of-the-first-occurrence-i.pattern_hint: The problem involves finding the index of the first occurrence of a substring, which suggests using a hashing approach to store the frequency of characters in the substring and the string. The goal is to find the index of the first occurrence, which hints at using a simple iteration approach.
find-the-index-of-the-first-occurrence-i.key_insight: The core idea is to use a simple iteration approach to compare the substring and the string, and then return the index of the first occurrence. This involves using a loop to iterate over the string and compare the substring, and then returning the index of the first occurrence.
find-the-index-of-the-first-occurrence-i.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard hashing template by requiring the use of a simple iteration approach, rather than using a hash map to store unique elements.
find-the-index-of-the-first-occurrence-i.common_mistake: A common mistake is to forget to consider the case where the substring is not found, which can lead to incorrect calculations.

solution_breakdown.intuition: We need to find the first occurrence of a smaller string (needle) within a larger string (haystack). A straightforward approach is to iterate through the haystack and, at each position, check if the substring starting from that position matches the needle.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem can be solved with a simple iterative approach, often referred to as a naive string matching algorithm. More advanced algorithms like KMP exist but are not necessary for typical interview constraints.

steps.title: Handle Edge Cases
steps.detail: If the needle is empty, return 0. If the haystack is shorter than the needle, return -1.

steps.title: Iterate Through Haystack
steps.detail: Loop through the haystack from the first character up to the last possible starting position for the needle.

steps.title: Compare Substring
steps.detail: At each position in the haystack, compare the substring of the haystack (starting at the current index and having the length of the needle) with the needle.

steps.title: Match Found
steps.detail: If the substring matches the needle, return the current index in the haystack.

steps.title: No Match Found
steps.detail: If the loop completes without finding a match, return -1.

edge_cases: Needle appears at the beginning of haystack.
Needle appears at the end of haystack.
Needle does not appear in haystack.

alternatives.tradeoff: Simplest and often most efficient in practice due to optimized native implementations, but doesn't demonstrate understanding of string searching algorithms.
More complex to implement but offers better time complexity (O(n+m)) by avoiding redundant comparisons using a precomputed failure function.
Uses hashing to compare substrings, offering average O(n+m) time complexity but can degrade to O(nm) in worst-case hash collisions.

non-decreasing-array.title: Non Decreasing Array
non-decreasing-array.description_html:
Given an array nums with n integers, your task is to check if it could become non-decreasing by modifying at most one element.

We define an array is non-decreasing if nums[i] <= nums[i + 1] holds for every i (0-based) such that (0 <= i <= n - 2).

 

Example 1:

Input: nums = [4,2,3]
Output: true
Explanation: You could modify the first 4 to 1 to get a non-decreasing array.

Example 2:

Input: nums = [4,2,1]
Output: false
Explanation: You cannot get a non-decreasing array by modifying at most one element.

 

Constraints:

  • n == nums.length
  • 1 <= n <= 104
  • -105 <= nums[i] <= 105
non-decreasing-array.description_text: Given an array nums with n integers, your task is to check if it could become non-decreasing by modifying at most one element. We define an array is non-decreasing if nums[i] <= nums[i + 1] holds for every i (0-based) such that ( 0 <= i <= n - 2 ). Example 1: Input: nums = [4,2,3] Output: true Explanation: You could modify the first 4 to 1 to get a non-decreasing array. Example 2: Input: nums = [4,2,1] Output: false Explanation: You cannot get a non-decreasing array by modifying at most one element. Constraints: n == nums.length 1 <= n <= 104 -105 <= nums[i] <= 105
non-decreasing-array.pattern_hint: The problem involves checking if an array is non-decreasing, which suggests using a simple iteration approach to compare adjacent elements. The goal is to find if the array can be made non-decreasing by changing at most one element, which hints at using a simple iteration approach.
non-decreasing-array.key_insight: The core idea is to use a simple iteration approach to compare adjacent elements and find the first decreasing pair, and then check if changing one element can make the array non-decreasing. This involves using a loop to iterate over the array and compare adjacent elements, and then checking if changing one element can make the array non-decreasing.
non-decreasing-array.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard hashing template by requiring the use of a simple iteration approach, rather than using a hash map to store unique elements.
non-decreasing-array.common_mistake: A common mistake is to forget to consider the case where changing one element can make the array non-decreasing, which can lead to incorrect calculations.

solution_breakdown.intuition: To make an array non-decreasing with at most one modification, we can iterate through the array and count the number of times the non-decreasing property is violated. If we find more than one violation, it's impossible. If we find one violation, we need to check if modifying one of the elements involved in the violation can fix it.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem involves iterating through an array and checking adjacent elements, which is a common pattern. The constraint of 'at most one modification' requires careful handling of violations.

steps.title: Initialize Violation Count
steps.detail: Initialize a counter for the number of violations to zero.

steps.title: Iterate and Check Violations
steps.detail: Iterate through the array from the second element, comparing each element with its previous one.

steps.title: Handle Violation
steps.detail: If `nums[i] < nums[i-1]`, increment the violation counter. If the counter exceeds 1, return `false` immediately.

steps.title: Check Modification Possibility
steps.detail: If a violation is found (i.e., `nums[i] < nums[i-1]`), check if modifying `nums[i]` or `nums[i-1]` can fix the non-decreasing property. This is possible if `i == 1` (modify `nums[i-1]`) or `nums[i] >= nums[i-2]` (modify `nums[i-1]`), or if `nums[i+1] >= nums[i-1]` (modify `nums[i]`). If neither modification works, return `false`.

steps.title: Handle Specific Violation Cases
steps.detail: If `nums[i] < nums[i-1]`, and it's the first violation: if `i == 1` or `nums[i] >= nums[i-2]`, we can potentially fix it by decreasing `nums[i-1]`. Otherwise, we must try to fix it by increasing `nums[i]` (which is implicitly handled by continuing the loop if `nums[i+1] >= nums[i-1]`).

steps.title: Return Result
steps.detail: If the loop completes and the violation count is 0 or 1 (and the single violation was fixable), return `true`.

edge_cases: Array with less than 2 elements (always true).
Array requires more than one modification.
Violation occurs at the beginning of the array.
Violation occurs at the end of the array.

alternatives.tradeoff: Inefficient as it would involve trying to modify each element and then checking the entire array, leading to O(n^2) complexity.

valid-palindrome-ii.title: Valid Palindrome II
valid-palindrome-ii.description_html:
Given a string s, return true if the s can be palindrome after deleting at most one character from it.

 

Example 1:

Input: s = "aba"
Output: true

Example 2:

Input: s = "abca"
Output: true
Explanation: You could delete the character 'c'.

Example 3:

Input: s = "abc"
Output: false

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= s.length <= 105
  • s consists of lowercase English letters.
valid-palindrome-ii.description_text: Given a string s , return true if the s can be palindrome after deleting at most one character from it. Example 1: Input: s = "aba" Output: true Example 2: Input: s = "abca" Output: true Explanation: You could delete the character 'c'. Example 3: Input: s = "abc" Output: false Constraints: 1 <= s.length <= 105 s consists of lowercase English letters.
valid-palindrome-ii.sub_pattern_name: Two Pointers Moving in Opposite Directions
valid-palindrome-ii.pattern_hint: The problem asks to determine if a string can be a palindrome by removing at most one character, which suggests using two pointers to compare characters from both ends. The presence of a condition that allows for one character removal indicates a variation of the two pointers technique.
valid-palindrome-ii.key_insight: The core idea is to use two pointers, one at the start and one at the end of the string, and move them towards each other, skipping one character if a mismatch is found. The key is to decide which pointer to move based on the comparison of the characters at the pointers and the characters next to them.
valid-palindrome-ii.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard two pointers template by introducing the possibility of removing one character, which requires additional logic to handle the removal and continue the comparison.
valid-palindrome-ii.common_mistake: A common mistake is not correctly handling the case when the removal of a character creates a new palindrome, or not considering all possible removals.

solution_breakdown.intuition: A palindrome reads the same forwards and backward. If a string can become a palindrome by removing at most one character, we can use two pointers starting from the ends and moving inwards. When a mismatch occurs, we have two choices: either skip the left character or skip the right character, and check if the remaining substring is a palindrome.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem utilizes the two-pointer technique, commonly used for palindrome checks, with a modification to handle the allowance of one character deletion.

steps.title: Initialize Pointers
steps.detail: Set up two pointers, `left` starting at the beginning (index 0) and `right` starting at the end of the string.

steps.title: Iterate and Compare
steps.detail: Move the pointers towards each other (`left++`, `right--`) as long as `left < right` and the characters at `s[left]` and `s[right]` match.

steps.title: Handle Mismatch
steps.detail: If `s[left]` does not equal `s[right]`, it means we've found a potential character to remove. We must check two possibilities: 1) If the substring from `left + 1` to `right` is a palindrome, or 2) If the substring from `left` to `right - 1` is a palindrome.

steps.title: Check Sub-Palindromes
steps.detail: Create a helper function `isPalindrome(substring)` that checks if a given substring is a palindrome using the standard two-pointer approach.

steps.title: Return Result
steps.detail: If either of the sub-palindrome checks returns true, the original string can be made a palindrome by removing one character, so return `true`. If the loop completes without finding a mismatch, the string is already a palindrome, so return `true`.

steps.title: Final Check
steps.detail: If a mismatch was found and neither of the sub-palindrome checks returned true, return `false`.

edge_cases: String with one character (is a palindrome).
String requires exactly one deletion to become a palindrome.
String requires more than one deletion to become a palindrome.

alternatives.tradeoff: Less efficient because it involves creating new strings and checking each one for palindrome property, leading to O(n^2) time complexity.

minimum-difference-between-highest-and-l.title: Minimum Difference Between Highest And Lowest of K Scores
minimum-difference-between-highest-and-l.description_html:
You are given a 0-indexed integer array nums, where nums[i] represents the score of the ith student. You are also given an integer k.

Pick the scores of any k students from the array so that the difference between the highest and the lowest of the k scores is minimized.

Return the minimum possible difference.

 

Example 1:

Input: nums = [90], k = 1
Output: 0
Explanation: There is one way to pick score(s) of one student:
- [90]. The difference between the highest and lowest score is 90 - 90 = 0.
The minimum possible difference is 0.

Example 2:

Input: nums = [9,4,1,7], k = 2
Output: 2
Explanation: There are six ways to pick score(s) of two students:
- [9,4,1,7]. The difference between the highest and lowest score is 9 - 4 = 5.
- [9,4,1,7]. The difference between the highest and lowest score is 9 - 1 = 8.
- [9,4,1,7]. The difference between the highest and lowest score is 9 - 7 = 2.
- [9,4,1,7]. The difference between the highest and lowest score is 4 - 1 = 3.
- [9,4,1,7]. The difference between the highest and lowest score is 7 - 4 = 3.
- [9,4,1,7]. The difference between the highest and lowest score is 7 - 1 = 6.
The minimum possible difference is 2.

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= k <= nums.length <= 1000
  • 0 <= nums[i] <= 105

Pick the scores of any k students from the array so that the difference between the highest and the lowest of the k scores is minimized. Return the minimum possible difference. minimum-difference-between-highest-and-l.sub_pattern_name: Two Pointers Moving in Opposite Directions
minimum-difference-between-highest-and-l.pattern_hint: The problem involves finding the minimum difference between the highest and lowest scores among any k consecutive scores, which suggests using a sliding window approach with two pointers. The requirement for k consecutive scores indicates a fixed-size window.
minimum-difference-between-highest-and-l.key_insight: The key idea is to maintain a window of k scores and calculate the difference between the highest and lowest scores within this window, then slide the window to the next set of k scores and repeat the calculation.
minimum-difference-between-highest-and-l.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard two pointers template by using a fixed-size window (k scores) instead of a variable-size window or pointers moving towards each other.
minimum-difference-between-highest-and-l.common_mistake: A common mistake is not correctly updating the minimum difference as the window slides, or not handling the edge case where the window reaches the end of the scores list.

solution_breakdown.intuition: To find the minimum difference between the highest and lowest of k scores, we need to consider all possible groups of k scores. If we sort the scores first, any group of k consecutive scores in the sorted list will contain the minimum and maximum values for that group. We can then use a sliding window of size k over the sorted array.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem is solved efficiently using sorting followed by a sliding window (or two-pointer) approach to examine contiguous subarrays of size k.

steps.title: Sort the Scores
steps.detail: Sort the input array `nums` in non-decreasing order.

steps.title: Initialize Minimum Difference
steps.detail: Initialize a variable `minDifference` to a very large value (e.g., `Integer.MAX_VALUE`).

steps.title: Slide Window
steps.detail: Iterate through the sorted array using a sliding window of size `k`. The window starts at index 0 and ends at index `k-1`, then slides one position at a time.

steps.title: Calculate Window Difference
steps.detail: For each window, the lowest score is `nums[i]` and the highest score is `nums[i + k - 1]`. Calculate the difference: `nums[i + k - 1] - nums[i]`.

steps.title: Update Minimum Difference
steps.detail: Update `minDifference` with the minimum value found so far: `minDifference = Math.min(minDifference, currentWindowDifference)`.

steps.title: Return Result
steps.detail: After iterating through all possible windows, return `minDifference`.

edge_cases: Array has fewer than k elements (problem constraints usually prevent this, but good to consider).
All elements in the array are the same.
The minimum difference occurs at the beginning of the sorted array.
The minimum difference occurs at the end of the sorted array.

alternatives.tradeoff: Extremely inefficient. Checking all combinations of k elements without sorting would be computationally expensive (e.g., using combinations logic).

contains-duplicate-ii.title: Contains Duplicate II
contains-duplicate-ii.description_html:
Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return true if there are two distinct indices i and j in the array such that nums[i] == nums[j] and abs(i - j) <= k.

 

Example 1:

Input: nums = [1,2,3,1], k = 3
Output: true

Example 2:

Input: nums = [1,0,1,1], k = 1
Output: true

Example 3:

Input: nums = [1,2,3,1,2,3], k = 2
Output: false

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= nums.length <= 105
  • -109 <= nums[i] <= 109
  • 0 <= k <= 105
contains-duplicate-ii.description_text: Given an integer array nums and an integer k , return true if there are two distinct indices i and j in the array such that nums[i] == nums[j] and abs(i - j) <= k . Example 1: Input: nums = [1,2,3,1], k = 3 Output: true Example 2: Input: nums = [1,0,1,1], k = 1 Output: true Example 3: Input: nums = [1,2,3,1,2,3], k = 2 Output: false Constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 105 -109 <= nums[i] <= 109 0 <= k <= 105
contains-duplicate-ii.pattern_hint: The problem asks to find if there are any duplicate elements within a certain distance (k) in the array, which suggests using a sliding window approach. The presence of a distance constraint (k) indicates a fixed-size window.
contains-duplicate-ii.key_insight: The core idea is to use a sliding window of size k and check for duplicates within this window, utilizing a data structure like a set to efficiently track unique elements within the current window.
contains-duplicate-ii.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard sliding window template by focusing on detecting duplicates within a fixed-size window rather than calculating a sum or average.
contains-duplicate-ii.common_mistake: A common mistake is not correctly updating the window boundaries or not removing elements that fall out of the window from the set of unique elements.

solution_breakdown.intuition: We need to find if there are duplicate numbers within a specific distance `k`. A sliding window approach is suitable here. We maintain a window of size `k+1` (or check elements within distance `k`) and use a set to keep track of the numbers currently in the window. If we encounter a number already in the set, we've found a duplicate within the distance.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem is a direct application of the sliding window technique, where the window size is determined by the distance constraint `k`.

steps.title: Initialize Data Structure
steps.detail: Use a HashSet to store elements within the current window.

steps.title: Iterate Through Array
steps.detail: Iterate through the input array `nums`.

steps.title: Check for Duplicates
steps.detail: For each element `nums[i]`, try to add it to the HashSet. If `add()` returns `false`, it means the element is already in the set, indicating a duplicate within the window. Return `true`.

steps.title: Maintain Window Size
steps.detail: If the current index `i` is greater than or equal to `k`, it means the element at `nums[i - k]` is now outside the window of distance `k`. Remove `nums[i - k]` from the HashSet.

steps.title: No Duplicate Found
steps.detail: If the loop completes without finding any duplicates within the distance `k`, return `false`.

edge_cases: k = 0 (no duplicates allowed unless the array has duplicates at the same index, which is impossible).
k is larger than or equal to the array length.
All elements are duplicates within distance k.
No duplicates exist in the array.

alternatives.tradeoff: Less efficient with O(n^2) time complexity, as it checks every pair of elements within distance k.
Similar efficiency to HashSet (O(n) time, O(min(n, k)) space) but stores indices, which might be useful for variations of the problem.

frequency-of-the-most-frequent-element.title: Frequency of The Most Frequent Element
frequency-of-the-most-frequent-element.description_html:
The frequency of an element is the number of times it occurs in an array.

You are given an integer array nums and an integer k. In one operation, you can choose an index of nums and increment the element at that index by 1.

Return the maximum possible frequency of an element after performing at most k operations.

 

Example 1:

Input: nums = [1,2,4], k = 5
Output: 3
Explanation: Increment the first element three times and the second element two times to make nums = [4,4,4].
4 has a frequency of 3.

Example 2:

Input: nums = [1,4,8,13], k = 5
Output: 2
Explanation: There are multiple optimal solutions:
- Increment the first element three times to make nums = [4,4,8,13]. 4 has a frequency of 2.
- Increment the second element four times to make nums = [1,8,8,13]. 8 has a frequency of 2.
- Increment the third element five times to make nums = [1,4,13,13]. 13 has a frequency of 2.

Example 3:

Input: nums = [3,9,6], k = 2
Output: 1

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= nums.length <= 105
  • 1 <= nums[i] <= 105
  • 1 <= k <= 105
frequency-of-the-most-frequent-element.description_text: The frequency of an element is the number of times it occurs in an array. You are given an integer array nums and an integer k . In one operation, you can choose an index of nums and increment the element at that index by 1 . Return the maximum possible frequency of an element after performing at most k operations. Example 1: Input: nums = [1,2,4], k = 5 Output: 3 Explanation: Increment the first element three times and the second element two times to make nums = [4,4,4]. 4 has a frequency of 3. Example 2: Input: nums = [1,4,8,13], k = 5 Output: 2 Explanation: There are multiple optimal solutions: - Increment the first element three times to make nums = [4,4,8,13]. 4 has a frequency of 2. - Increment the second element four times to make nums = [1,8,8,13]. 8 has a frequency of 2. - Increment the third element five times to make nums = [1,4,13,13]. 13 has a frequency of 2. Example 3: Input: nums = [3,9,6], k = 2 Output: 1 Constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 105 1 <= nums[i] <= 105 1 <= k <= 105
frequency-of-the-most-frequent-element.pattern_hint: The problem involves maximizing the frequency of the most frequent element in an array by adding k elements, which suggests using a sliding window approach to identify the most frequent element and calculate the maximum frequency. The presence of a constraint (k) indicates a variable-size window.
frequency-of-the-most-frequent-element.key_insight: The key idea is to use a sliding window to identify the most frequent element and calculate the maximum frequency achievable by adding k elements, considering the size of the window and the frequency of the most frequent element within it.
frequency-of-the-most-frequent-element.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard sliding window template by focusing on maximizing the frequency of the most frequent element rather than calculating a sum or average, and by considering the addition of elements to achieve this goal.
frequency-of-the-most-frequent-element.common_mistake: A common mistake is not correctly calculating the maximum frequency achievable by adding k elements, or not considering the optimal size of the window to maximize the frequency.

solution_breakdown.intuition: We want to maximize the frequency of an element by incrementing other elements. This means we are looking for a subarray where all elements can be made equal to the largest element in that subarray using at most `k` increments. Sorting the array first allows us to use a sliding window, as any potential subarray will have its elements clustered together after sorting.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem is solved using a sliding window approach on a sorted array. The window size is variable, and we expand it as long as the condition (total increments needed <= k) is met.

steps.title: Sort the Array
steps.detail: Sort the input array `nums` in non-decreasing order. This is crucial because we want to make elements equal to the largest element in a potential subarray.

steps.title: Initialize Variables
steps.detail: Initialize `left = 0`, `maxFrequency = 0`, `currentSum = 0` (sum of elements in the current window).

steps.title: Iterate and Expand Window
steps.detail: Iterate through the sorted array with a `right` pointer from 0 to `nums.length - 1`.

steps.title: Update Window Sum
steps.detail: Add the current element `nums[right]` to `currentSum`.

steps.title: Check Condition and Shrink Window
steps.detail: Calculate the required increments: `(nums[right] * (right - left + 1)) - currentSum`. If this value exceeds `k`, it means we need too many increments. Shrink the window from the left by subtracting `nums[left]` from `currentSum` and incrementing `left`.

steps.title: Update Max Frequency
steps.detail: After potentially shrinking the window, the current window size (`right - left + 1`) represents a valid frequency achievable with at most `k` increments. Update `maxFrequency = Math.max(maxFrequency, right - left + 1)`.

steps.title: Return Result
steps.detail: After the loop finishes, return `maxFrequency`.

edge_cases: The optimal subarray is at the beginning of the sorted array.
The optimal subarray is at the end of the sorted array.
Large values of k that allow all elements to be made equal.

alternatives.tradeoff: Inefficient. Iterating through all possible subarrays and checking the increment condition for each would lead to O(n^2) or O(n^3) complexity.

guess-number-higher-or-lower.title: Guess Number Higher Or Lower
guess-number-higher-or-lower.description_html:
We are playing the Guess Game. The game is as follows:

I pick a number from 1 to n. You have to guess which number I picked (the number I picked stays the same throughout the game).

Every time you guess wrong, I will tell you whether the number I picked is higher or lower than your guess.

You call a pre-defined API int guess(int num), which returns three possible results:

  • -1: Your guess is higher than the number I picked (i.e. num > pick).
  • 1: Your guess is lower than the number I picked (i.e. num < pick).
  • 0: your guess is equal to the number I picked (i.e. num == pick).

Return the number that I picked.

 

Example 1:

Input: n = 10, pick = 6
Output: 6

Example 2:

Input: n = 1, pick = 1
Output: 1

Example 3:

Input: n = 2, pick = 1
Output: 1

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= n <= 231 - 1
  • 1 <= pick <= n
guess-number-higher-or-lower.description_text: We are playing the Guess Game. The game is as follows: I pick a number from 1 to n . You have to guess which number I picked (the number I picked stays the same throughout the game). Every time you guess wrong, I will tell you whether the number I picked is higher or lower than your guess. You call a pre-defined API int guess(int num) , which returns three possible results: -1 : Your guess is higher than the number I picked (i.e. num > pick ). 1 : Your guess is lower than the number I picked (i.e. num < pick ). 0 : your guess is equal to the number I picked (i.e. num == pick ). Return the number that I picked. Example 1: Input: n = 10, pick = 6 Output: 6 Example 2: Input: n = 1, pick = 1 Output: 1 Example 3: Input: n = 2, pick = 1 Output: 1 Constraints: 1 <= n <= 231 - 1 1 <= pick <= n
guess-number-higher-or-lower.pattern_hint: The problem involves guessing a number within a range by repeatedly asking if the guess is higher or lower than the target number, which suggests using a binary search approach. The presence of feedback (higher or lower) indicates a divide-and-conquer strategy.
guess-number-higher-or-lower.key_insight: The core idea is to use binary search to divide the search space in half with each guess, adjusting the range based on the feedback (higher or lower) to converge on the target number.
guess-number-higher-or-lower.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard binary search template by using a dynamic range that changes with each guess, rather than a fixed array or list.
guess-number-higher-or-lower.common_mistake: A common mistake is not correctly updating the search range based on the feedback, or not using the optimal midpoint as the next guess.

solution_breakdown.intuition: The problem involves finding a hidden number within a given range by making guesses and receiving feedback ('higher' or 'lower'). This is a classic scenario for binary search, where we repeatedly narrow down the search space by choosing the middle element as our guess.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem is a direct application of the binary search algorithm, specifically tailored for a search space where comparisons yield directional feedback.

steps.title: Initialize Search Range
steps.detail: Set the lower bound `low` to 1 and the upper bound `high` to the given number `n`.

steps.title: Binary Search Loop
steps.detail: While `low <= high`, continue the search.

steps.title: Calculate Midpoint
steps.detail: Calculate the middle index `mid = low + (high - low) / 2`. Using this form prevents potential integer overflow compared to `(low + high) / 2`.

steps.title: Make Guess and Get Feedback
steps.detail: Call the `guess(mid)` function, which returns -1 if `pick` < `mid`, 1 if `pick` > `mid`, and 0 if `pick` == `mid`.

steps.title: Adjust Search Range
steps.detail: If `guess(mid)` returns 0, we found the number, return `mid`. If it returns 1 (`pick` > `mid`), the target number is in the upper half, so update `low = mid + 1`. If it returns -1 (`pick` < `mid`), the target number is in the lower half, so update `high = mid - 1`.

steps.title: Return Result
steps.detail: If the loop terminates without finding the number (which shouldn't happen given the problem constraints), return -1 or handle appropriately.

edge_cases: The range [1, n] is very large, potentially causing integer overflow if midpoint is not calculated carefully.
The `guess` API always returns the correct feedback.

alternatives.tradeoff: Much less efficient. It would involve checking every number from 1 to n sequentially, resulting in O(n) time complexity, which is too slow for large values of n.

k-closest-points-to-origin.title: K Closest Points to Origin
k-closest-points-to-origin.description_html:
Given an array of points where points[i] = [xi, yi] represents a point on the X-Y plane and an integer k, return the k closest points to the origin (0, 0).

The distance between two points on the X-Y plane is the Euclidean distance (i.e., √(x1 - x2)2 + (y1 - y2)2).

You may return the answer in any order. The answer is guaranteed to be unique (except for the order that it is in).

 

Example 1:

Input: points = [[1,3],[-2,2]], k = 1
Output: [[-2,2]]
Explanation:
The distance between (1, 3) and the origin is sqrt(10).
The distance between (-2, 2) and the origin is sqrt(8).
Since sqrt(8) < sqrt(10), (-2, 2) is closer to the origin.
We only want the closest k = 1 points from the origin, so the answer is just [[-2,2]].

Example 2:

Input: points = [[3,3],[5,-1],[-2,4]], k = 2
Output: [[3,3],[-2,4]]
Explanation: The answer [[-2,4],[3,3]] would also be accepted.

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= k <= points.length <= 104
  • -104 <= xi, yi <= 104
k-closest-points-to-origin.description_text: Given an array of points where points[i] = [xi, yi] represents a point on the X-Y plane and an integer k , return the k closest points to the origin (0, 0) . The distance between two points on the X-Y plane is the Euclidean distance (i.e., √(x1 - x2)2 + (y1 - y2)2 ). You may return the answer in any order. The answer is guaranteed to be unique (except for the order that it is in). Example 1: Input: points = [[1,3],[-2,2]], k = 1 Output: [[-2,2]] Explanation: The distance between (1, 3) and the origin is sqrt(10). The distance between (-2, 2) and the origin is sqrt(8). Since sqrt(8) < sqrt(10), (-2, 2) is closer to the origin. We only want the closest k = 1 points from the origin, so the answer is just [[-2,2]]. Example 2: Input: points = [[3,3],[5,-1],[-2,4]], k = 2 Output: [[3,3],[-2,4]] Explanation: The answer [[-2,4],[3,3]] would also be accepted. Constraints: 1 <= k <= points.length <= 104 -104 <= xi, yi <= 104
k-closest-points-to-origin.pattern_hint: The problem asks for the 'K Closest' points, indicating a need to prioritize or rank items, which is a clear signal for using a Heap or Priority Queue. The distance formula also hints at a comparison-based approach.
k-closest-points-to-origin.key_insight: The core idea is to use a max heap to store points based on their distance from the origin, and then extract the K smallest elements. The key is to define a custom comparator for the heap.
k-closest-points-to-origin.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard heap template by requiring a custom distance calculation as the comparison metric.
k-closest-points-to-origin.common_mistake: A common mistake is to calculate the distance incorrectly or to forget to consider the case where two points have the same distance from the origin.

solution_breakdown.intuition: The problem asks for the 'K Closest' points, indicating a need to prioritize or rank items based on their distance from the origin. A max heap is ideal here because we want to keep track of the K closest points seen so far, and efficiently discard points that are farther away.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem directly utilizes the Heap/Priority Queue pattern by maintaining a collection of the K closest points encountered. The heap allows for efficient insertion and removal of elements based on their distance.

steps.title: Initialize a Max Heap
steps.detail: Create a max heap that stores points, ordered by their squared Euclidean distance from the origin.

steps.title: Iterate Through Points
steps.detail: For each point in the input array, calculate its squared distance from the origin.

steps.title: Add to Heap
steps.detail: Add the current point and its distance to the max heap.

steps.title: Maintain Heap Size
steps.detail: If the heap size exceeds K, remove the element with the largest distance (the root of the max heap).

steps.title: Extract Result
steps.detail: After processing all points, the heap will contain the K closest points; extract them into a result array.

edge_cases: K is equal to the total number of points.
All points are at the same distance from the origin.
The input array of points is empty.

alternatives.tradeoff: Sorting all points by distance and taking the first K is simpler to implement but less efficient (O(N log N)) compared to the heap approach (O(N log K)).
QuickSelect offers an average time complexity of O(N) but has a worst-case complexity of O(N^2), making the heap approach more consistently performant.

task-scheduler.title: Task Scheduler
task-scheduler.description_html:
You are given an array of CPU tasks, each labeled with a letter from A to Z, and a number n. Each CPU interval can be idle or allow the completion of one task. Tasks can be completed in any order, but there's a constraint: there has to be a gap of at least n intervals between two tasks with the same label.

Return the minimum number of CPU intervals required to complete all tasks.

 

Example 1:

Input: tasks = ["A","A","A","B","B","B"], n = 2

Output: 8

Explanation: A possible sequence is: A -> B -> idle -> A -> B -> idle -> A -> B.

After completing task A, you must wait two intervals before doing A again. The same applies to task B. In the 3rd interval, neither A nor B can be done, so you idle. By the 4th interval, you can do A again as 2 intervals have passed.

Example 2:

Input: tasks = ["A","C","A","B","D","B"], n = 1

Output: 6

Explanation: A possible sequence is: A -> B -> C -> D -> A -> B.

With a cooling interval of 1, you can repeat a task after just one other task.

Example 3:

Input: tasks = ["A","A","A", "B","B","B"], n = 3

Output: 10

Explanation: A possible sequence is: A -> B -> idle -> idle -> A -> B -> idle -> idle -> A -> B.

There are only two types of tasks, A and B, which need to be separated by 3 intervals. This leads to idling twice between repetitions of these tasks.

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= tasks.length <= 104
  • tasks[i] is an uppercase English letter.
  • 0 <= n <= 100
task-scheduler.description_text: You are given an array of CPU tasks , each labeled with a letter from A to Z, and a number n . Each CPU interval can be idle or allow the completion of one task. Tasks can be completed in any order, but there's a constraint: there has to be a gap of at least n intervals between two tasks with the same label. Return the minimum number of CPU intervals required to complete all tasks. Example 1: Input: tasks = ["A","A","A","B","B","B"], n = 2 Output: 8 Explanation: A possible sequence is: A -> B -> idle -> A -> B -> idle -> A -> B. After completing task A, you must wait two intervals before doing A again. The same applies to task B. In the 3rd interval, neither A nor B can be done, so you idle. By the 4th interval, you can do A again as 2 intervals have passed. Example 2: Input: tasks = ["A","C","A","B","D","B"], n = 1 Output: 6 Explanation: A possible sequence is: A -> B -> C -> D -> A -> B. With a cooling interval of 1, you can repeat a task after just one other task. Example 3: Input: tasks = ["A","A","A", "B","B","B"], n = 3 Output: 10 Explanation: A possible sequence is: A -> B -> idle -> idle -> A -> B -> idle -> idle -> A -> B. There are only two types of tasks, A and B, which need to be separated by 3 intervals. This leads to idling twice between repetitions of these tasks. Constraints: 1 <= tasks.length <= 104 tasks[i] is an uppercase English letter. 0 <= n <= 100
task-scheduler.pattern_hint: The problem involves scheduling tasks with a cooldown period, which suggests the need to manage a queue or priority of tasks based on their cooldown status. The requirement to find the least number of units of time indicates an optimization problem.
task-scheduler.key_insight: The key insight is to use a max heap to store tasks based on their cooldown period, and a queue to manage the tasks that are cooling down. The max heap ensures that the task with the longest cooldown period is executed first.
task-scheduler.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard heap template by requiring a combination of a max heap and a queue to manage the cooldown periods.
task-scheduler.common_mistake: A common mistake is to forget to consider the case where multiple tasks have the same cooldown period.

solution_breakdown.intuition: To minimize the total time, we should prioritize executing tasks that have the longest cooldown periods or are most frequent. A max heap helps us pick the most 'urgent' task, while a queue manages tasks currently in their cooldown period.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem is a classic application of the Heap/Priority Queue pattern, combined with a queue for managing cooldowns. The heap prioritizes tasks, and the queue ensures the cooldown constraint is met.

steps.title: Count Task Frequencies
steps.detail: Create a frequency map (or array) to count the occurrences of each task.

steps.title: Initialize Max Heap
steps.detail: Populate a max heap with the frequencies of all tasks, ordered by frequency.

steps.title: Simulate Scheduling
steps.detail: Use a loop to simulate time units, processing tasks in cycles.

steps.title: Process Tasks in a Cycle
steps.detail: In each cycle (of length n+1, where n is the cooldown), extract up to n+1 tasks from the heap, decrement their counts, and add them to a temporary list.

steps.title: Re-add Tasks
steps.detail: After processing a cycle, re-add tasks from the temporary list back into the heap if their counts are still greater than zero.

steps.title: Calculate Total Time
steps.detail: The total time is the sum of full cycles plus the number of tasks processed in the last, potentially incomplete, cycle.

edge_cases: No tasks are given (empty input array).
The number of tasks is less than the cooldown period.

alternatives.tradeoff: This approach calculates the result directly using the most frequent task and the cooldown period, which is very efficient (O(N)) but less intuitive to derive than the heap-based simulation.

word-ladder.title: Word Ladder
word-ladder.description_html:
A transformation sequence from word beginWord to word endWord using a dictionary wordList is a sequence of words beginWord -> s1 -> s2 -> ... -> sk such that:

  • Every adjacent pair of words differs by a single letter.
  • Every si for 1 <= i <= k is in wordList. Note that beginWord does not need to be in wordList.
  • sk == endWord

Given two words, beginWord and endWord, and a dictionary wordList, return the number of words in the shortest transformation sequence from beginWord to endWord, or 0 if no such sequence exists.

 

Example 1:

Input: beginWord = "hit", endWord = "cog", wordList = ["hot","dot","dog","lot","log","cog"]
Output: 5
Explanation: One shortest transformation sequence is "hit" -> "hot" -> "dot" -> "dog" -> cog", which is 5 words long.

Example 2:

Input: beginWord = "hit", endWord = "cog", wordList = ["hot","dot","dog","lot","log"]
Output: 0
Explanation: The endWord "cog" is not in wordList, therefore there is no valid transformation sequence.

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= beginWord.length <= 10
  • endWord.length == beginWord.length
  • 1 <= wordList.length <= 5000
  • wordList[i].length == beginWord.length
  • beginWord, endWord, and wordList[i] consist of lowercase English letters.
  • beginWord != endWord
  • All the words in wordList are unique.
word-ladder.description_text: A transformation sequence from word beginWord to word endWord using a dictionary wordList is a sequence of words beginWord -> s1 -> s2 -> ... -> sk such that: Every adjacent pair of words differs by a single letter. Every si for 1 <= i <= k is in wordList . Note that beginWord does not need to be in wordList . sk == endWord Given two words, beginWord and endWord , and a dictionary wordList , return the number of words in the shortest transformation sequence from beginWord to endWord , or 0 if no such sequence exists. Example 1: Input: beginWord = "hit", endWord = "cog", wordList = ["hot","dot","dog","lot","log","cog"] Output: 5 Explanation: One shortest transformation sequence is "hit" -> "hot" -> "dot" -> "dog" -> cog", which is 5 words long. Example 2: Input: beginWord = "hit", endWord = "cog", wordList = ["hot","dot","dog","lot","log"] Output: 0 Explanation: The endWord "cog" is not in wordList, therefore there is no valid transformation sequence. Constraints: 1 <= beginWord.length <= 10 endWord.length == beginWord.length 1 <= wordList.length <= 5000 wordList[i].length == beginWord.length beginWord , endWord , and wordList[i] consist of lowercase English letters. beginWord != endWord All the words in wordList are unique.
word-ladder.sub_pattern_name: Graph Shortest Paths and Word Ladders
word-ladder.pattern_hint: The problem involves transforming one word into another by changing one character at a time, which suggests a graph-based approach where each word is a node and the edges represent possible transformations.
word-ladder.key_insight: The core idea is to use a breadth-first search (BFS) algorithm to traverse the graph of word transformations, starting from the given word and exploring all possible transformations until the target word is found.
word-ladder.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard graph template by requiring a custom graph construction based on word transformations.
word-ladder.common_mistake: A common mistake is to forget to consider the case where a word has multiple possible transformations.

solution_breakdown.intuition: We are looking for the shortest sequence of transformations, which suggests exploring possibilities level by level. This is a classic Breadth-First Search (BFS) scenario where each word is a node and transformations are edges.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem is a prime example of applying graph traversal, specifically BFS, to find the shortest path in an unweighted graph. The words form the nodes, and possible one-character transformations form the edges.

steps.title: Build Adjacency List (Implicitly)
steps.detail: Create a set of all words in the dictionary for efficient lookups.

steps.title: Initialize BFS
steps.detail: Start a BFS with a queue containing the beginWord and a visited set to track visited words.

steps.title: Level-by-Level Traversal
steps.detail: Process words level by level, where each level represents one transformation step.

steps.title: Generate Neighbors
steps.detail: For each word, generate all possible one-character transformations.

steps.title: Check Neighbors
steps.detail: If a generated word is in the dictionary and not visited, add it to the queue and mark it as visited.

steps.title: Termination Condition
steps.detail: If the endWord is found, return the current level (path length); otherwise, continue until the queue is empty (return 0 if endWord is unreachable).

edge_cases: beginWord and endWord are the same.
endWord is not in the wordList.
No transformation path exists between beginWord and endWord.
beginWord is not in the wordList (but can be transformed into words in the list).

alternatives.tradeoff: DFS can find a path but is not guaranteed to find the shortest path without modifications (like iterative deepening), making BFS the more natural choice for shortest path problems.
Starting BFS from both beginWord and endWord simultaneously can significantly reduce the search space and improve performance, especially for large word lists.

reconstruct-itinerary.title: Reconstruct Itinerary
reconstruct-itinerary.description_html:
You are given a list of airline tickets where tickets[i] = [fromi, toi] represent the departure and the arrival airports of one flight. Reconstruct the itinerary in order and return it.

All of the tickets belong to a man who departs from "JFK", thus, the itinerary must begin with "JFK". If there are multiple valid itineraries, you should return the itinerary that has the smallest lexical order when read as a single string.

  • For example, the itinerary ["JFK", "LGA"] has a smaller lexical order than ["JFK", "LGB"].

You may assume all tickets form at least one valid itinerary. You must use all the tickets once and only once.

 

Example 1:

Input: tickets = [["MUC","LHR"],["JFK","MUC"],["SFO","SJC"],["LHR","SFO"]]
Output: ["JFK","MUC","LHR","SFO","SJC"]

Example 2:

Input: tickets = [["JFK","SFO"],["JFK","ATL"],["SFO","ATL"],["ATL","JFK"],["ATL","SFO"]]
Output: ["JFK","ATL","JFK","SFO","ATL","SFO"]
Explanation: Another possible reconstruction is ["JFK","SFO","ATL","JFK","ATL","SFO"] but it is larger in lexical order.

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= tickets.length <= 300
  • tickets[i].length == 2
  • fromi.length == 3
  • toi.length == 3
  • fromi and toi consist of uppercase English letters.
  • fromi != toi
reconstruct-itinerary.description_text: You are given a list of airline tickets where tickets[i] = [fromi, toi] represent the departure and the arrival airports of one flight. Reconstruct the itinerary in order and return it. All of the tickets belong to a man who departs from "JFK" , thus, the itinerary must begin with "JFK" . If there are multiple valid itineraries, you should return the itinerary that has the smallest lexical order when read as a single string. For example, the itinerary ["JFK", "LGA"] has a smaller lexical order than ["JFK", "LGB"] . You may assume all tickets form at least one valid itinerary. You must use all the tickets once and only once. Example 1: Input: tickets = [["MUC","LHR"],["JFK","MUC"],["SFO","SJC"],["LHR","SFO"]] Output: ["JFK","MUC","LHR","SFO","SJC"] Example 2: Input: tickets = [["JFK","SFO"],["JFK","ATL"],["SFO","ATL"],["ATL","JFK"],["ATL","SFO"]] Output: ["JFK","ATL","JFK","SFO","ATL","SFO"] Explanation: Another possible reconstruction is ["JFK","SFO","ATL","JFK","ATL","SFO"] but it is larger in lexical order. Constraints: 1 <= tickets.length <= 300 tickets[i].length == 2 fromi.length == 3 toi.length == 3 fromi and toi consist of uppercase English letters. fromi != toi
reconstruct-itinerary.pattern_hint: The problem involves reconstructing a flight itinerary from a given list of tickets, which suggests a graph-based approach where each city is a node and the edges represent flights.
reconstruct-itinerary.key_insight: The key insight is to use a depth-first search (DFS) algorithm to traverse the graph of flights, starting from a given city and exploring all possible itineraries until a valid one is found.
reconstruct-itinerary.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard graph template by requiring a custom graph construction based on flight tickets and a DFS approach to find the correct itinerary.
reconstruct-itinerary.common_mistake: A common mistake is to forget to consider the case where a city has multiple outgoing flights.

solution_breakdown.intuition: We need to find a specific path (itinerary) through a set of flights, starting from a given airport. This suggests a graph traversal where airports are nodes and flights are directed edges, and we need to find a valid Eulerian path.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem is a graph traversal problem that can be solved using Depth First Search (DFS), specifically tailored to find an Eulerian path. The airports represent nodes, and flights represent directed edges.

steps.title: Build Adjacency List
steps.detail: Create a map where keys are departure airports and values are lists of destination airports, sorted lexicographically.

steps.title: Initialize DFS
steps.detail: Start a DFS from the 'JFK' airport.

steps.title: Recursive Traversal
steps.detail: Recursively visit destinations. For each flight from the current airport, take the lexicographically smallest destination first.

steps.title: Mark Visited Edges
steps.detail: Remove the used flight (edge) from the adjacency list to avoid reusing it.

steps.title: Backtrack and Add to Result
steps.detail: After visiting all reachable destinations from a node, add the current node to the beginning of the result list (post-order traversal).

steps.title: Return Itinerary
steps.detail: The final result list, when reversed, will represent the reconstructed itinerary.

edge_cases: Multiple flights between the same two airports.
A city has multiple outgoing flights.
The input list of tickets is empty.

alternatives.tradeoff: An iterative approach using a stack can avoid potential stack overflow errors for very deep recursion, but the logic can be slightly more complex to manage than the recursive DFS.

While the provided DFS implicitly implements Hierholzer's algorithm for finding Eulerian paths, explicitly implementing it might offer clearer separation of concerns but requires careful management of visited edges.

min-cost-to-connect-all-points.title: Min Cost to Connect All Points
min-cost-to-connect-all-points.description_html:
You are given an array points representing integer coordinates of some points on a 2D-plane, where points[i] = [xi, yi].

The cost of connecting two points [xi, yi] and [xj, yj] is the manhattan distance between them: |xi - xj| + |yi - yj|, where |val| denotes the absolute value of val.

Return the minimum cost to make all points connected. All points are connected if there is exactly one simple path between any two points.

 

Example 1:

Input: points = [[0,0],[2,2],[3,10],[5,2],[7,0]]
Output: 20
Explanation: 

We can connect the points as shown above to get the minimum cost of 20.
Notice that there is a unique path between every pair of points.

Example 2:

Input: points = [[3,12],[-2,5],[-4,1]]
Output: 18

 

Constraints:

  • 1 <= points.length <= 1000
  • -106 <= xi, yi <= 106
  • All pairs (xi, yi) are distinct.
min-cost-to-connect-all-points.description_text: You are given an array points representing integer coordinates of some points on a 2D-plane, where points[i] = [xi, yi] . The cost of connecting two points [xi, yi] and [xj, yj] is the manhattan distance between them: |xi - xj| + |yi - yj| , where |val| denotes the absolute value of val . Return the minimum cost to make all points connected. All points are connected if there is exactly one simple path between any two points. Example 1: Input: points = [[0,0],[2,2],[3,10],[5,2],[7,0]] Output: 20 Explanation: We can connect the points as shown above to get the minimum cost of 20. Notice that there is a unique path between every pair of points. Example 2: Input: points = [[3,12],[-2,5],[-4,1]] Output: 18 Constraints: 1 <= points.length <= 1000 -106 <= xi, yi <= 106 All pairs (xi, yi) are distinct.
min-cost-to-connect-all-points.pattern_hint: The problem involves connecting a set of points with the minimum total cost, which suggests a graph-based approach where each point is a node and the edges represent possible connections.
min-cost-to-connect-all-points.key_insight: The core idea is to use a minimum spanning tree (MST) algorithm, such as Kruskal's or Prim's, to find the optimal set of connections that minimize the total cost.
min-cost-to-connect-all-points.template_deviation: This problem deviates from the standard graph template by requiring a custom edge weight calculation based on the distance between points.
min-cost-to-connect-all-points.common_mistake: A common mistake is to forget to consider the case where a point has multiple possible connections.

solution_breakdown.intuition: We want to connect all points with the minimum total cost, which is a classic Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) problem. We can view each point as a node in a graph, and the cost between any two points as the weight of the edge connecting them.
solution_breakdown.pattern_connection: This problem directly maps to finding a Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) in a complete graph. Algorithms like Prim's or Kruskal's are suitable for finding the MST.

steps.title: Represent Points as Graph Nodes
steps.detail: Consider each point as a node in a graph.

steps.title: Calculate All Edge Weights
steps.detail: Compute the Manhattan distance between every pair of points; these distances represent the edge weights.

steps.title: Initialize MST Algorithm
steps.detail: Choose either Prim's or Kruskal's algorithm. For Kruskal's, create a list of all edges (point pairs and their distances). For Prim's, start with an arbitrary point.

steps.title: Build the MST
steps.detail: Apply the chosen MST algorithm (Prim's or Kruskal's) to select edges that connect all points with the minimum total weight, ensuring no cycles are formed.

steps.title: Sum Edge Weights
steps.detail: Sum the weights of the edges included in the MST to get the minimum cost.

edge_cases: All points are at the same location.
The number of points is large.

alternatives.tradeoff: Kruskal's algorithm involves sorting all edges first (O(E log E)) and then using a Union-Find data structure. Prim's algorithm, especially with a priority queue, can be more efficient for dense graphs (like the complete graph here) with a complexity of O(E log V) or O(N^2 log N) if implemented naively.
Trying all possible subsets of edges to connect points would be computationally infeasible (exponential time complexity) and is not a practical solution.

swim-in-rising-water.title: Swim In Rising Water
swim-in-rising-water.description_html:
You are given an n x n integer matrix grid where each value grid[i][j] represents the elevation at that point (i, j).

It starts raining, and water gradually rises over time. At time t, the water level is t, meaning any cell with elevation less than equal to t is submerged or reachable.

You can swim from a square to another 4-directionally adjacent square if and only if the elevation of both squares individually are at most t. You can swim infinite distances in zero time. Of course, you must stay within the boundaries of the grid during your swim.

Return the minimum time until you can reach the bottom right square (n - 1, n - 1) if you start at the top left square (0, 0).

 

Example 1:

Input: grid = [[0,2],[1,3]]
Output: 3
Explanation:
At time 0, you are in grid location (0, 0).
You cannot go anywhere else because 4-directionally adjacent neighbors have a higher elevation than t = 0.
You cannot reach point (1, 1) until time 3.
When the depth of water is 3, we can swim anywhere inside the grid.

Example 2:

Input: grid = [[0,1,2,3,4],[24,23,22,21,5],[12,13,14,15,16],[11,17,18,19,20],[10,9,8,7,6]]
Output: 16
Explanation: The final route is shown.
We need to wait until time 16 so that (0, 0) and (4, 4) are connected.

 

Constraints:

  • n == grid.length
  • n == grid[i].length
  • 1 <= n <= 50
  • 0 <= grid[i][j] < n2
  • Each value grid[i][j] is unique.
swim-in-rising-water.description_text: You are given an n x n integer matrix grid where each value grid[i][j] represents the elevation at that point (i, j) . It starts raining, and water gradually rises over time. At time t , the water level is t , meaning any cell with elevation less than equal to t is submerged or reachable. You can swim from a square to another 4-directionally adjacent square if and only if the elevation of both squares individually are at most t . You can swim infinite distances in zero time. Of course, you must stay within the boundaries of the grid during your swim. Return the minimum time until you can reach the bottom right square (n - 1, n - 1) if you start at the top left square (0, 0) . Example 1: Input: grid = [[0,2],[1,3]] Output: 3 Explanation: At time 0, you are in grid location (0, 0). You cannot go anywhere else because 4-directionally adjacent neighbors have a higher elevation than t = 0. You cannot reach point (1, 1) until time 3. When the depth of water is 3, we can swim anywhere inside the grid. Example 2: Input: grid = [[0,1,2,3,4],[24,23,22,21,5],[12,13,14,15,16],[11,17,18,19,20],[10,9,8,7,6]] Output: 16 Explanation: The final route is shown. We need to wait until time 16 so that (0, 0) and (4, 4) are connected. Constraints: n == grid.length n == grid[i].length 1 <= n <= 50 0 <= grid[i][j] < n2 Each value grid[i][j] is unique.
